The existing data model requires every purchase task to have a . Purchases that do not belong to any real project, like conference flights, lab snacks, or one-off equipment, still need somewhere to land. Rather than making nullable and updating every reader (Gantt, Workbench, ...

The hidden project never appears on Home, Workbench, Gantt, the project picker, or anywhere in the app that calls without explicitly opting in. The route is the only surface that passes , so the bucket is visible (and filterable) there but invisible everywhere else.

Lab purchasing has heavy vendor reuse. The same supplier shows up across dozens of orders, often spelled three different ways. To make that data useful for analytics, every line item now carries a vendor and a category , both stored as nullable free-text strings on . They are ...

Each input is wired to a sourced from , deduplicated, non-null values only. So the first time you type into a vendor cell, the dropdown surfaces every distinct vendor you (or anyone sharing with you) has typed before. Suggestions update as new values are added elsewhere, inclu...

A purchase is not done the moment you log it. Every line item carries an ordering status that moves through three stages, Needs ordering , then Ordered , then Received . A fresh item starts at Needs ordering, and older records with no status read as Needs ordering too, so the ...

Each line item shows a small colored status chip (gray for Needs ordering, blue for Ordered, green for Received) with forward and back arrows next to it. Click the forward arrow to advance a stage or the back arrow to revert one. The control is available to any lab member, so ...

Reagents run out and get reordered on a loop. On any Received line item, a one-click Buy again control creates a brand-new purchase order that copies the item's name, vendor, CAS or accession, link, price, and quantity, and starts it back at Needs ordering . No form, no retypi...

ResearchOS does not block you from attaching a purchase item to a task whose type is not . That is on purpose. Reagents often tie to a specific experiment task, and you don't want to lose that experiment-to-spend link.

Opening a purchase editor against a non-purchase task surfaces a soft amber note above the line-item table. The system does not block you. The items will show up under Items on non-purchase tasks in the dashboard below so they stay visible.

Scroll past the order list and the page closes out with a live spending dashboard. Every number on it is recomputed from the same set of items you can see above. The on-disk field is known-stale and is not used here. Live computation avoids drift bugs when an item is edited or...

The top of the dashboard has two controls. The time range dropdown chooses the window: Last 30 days , Last 90 days , Last 12 months (the default), All time , or a Custom date range with from and to inputs. The All projects checkbox lets the dashboard ignore the global project ...

Below the controls is a row of cards, one per funding account. Each card shows the account name, dollars spent, total budget, dollars remaining, and a progress bar. The numbers are computed live from items in the current window. Over-budget cards turn red. Items with a funding...

Next is a vertical bar chart of monthly spend across the selected window. Empty months still render as zero-height bars so the time span on the x-axis matches your range selection. Hover any bar to see the dollar total and item count for that month.

Below the time chart is a horizontal bar chart with a segmented control in the section header to switch lenses. The same set of items in the current window is regrouped by project, by vendor, or by category. Items with no value for the active lens (no vendor set, no category s...

Near the bottom of the dashboard is a thin amber line that reads Items on non-purchase tasks: followed by a count and a dollar total. Click it to expand an inline table of those items with their host task name and amount. This panel exists because the loose model lets you legi...

The top-right of the dashboard has an Export CSV button that downloads the currently filtered scope (time range plus project filter, plus any funding-card filter you have clicked) as a flat file named . Columns are item id, item name, vendor, category, funding string, project ...

Next to the export button, PIs see a Open in Lab Overview shortcut that opens the same purchase data inside the LabPurchases Tool popup on /lab-overview , where the funding-bar, burn-rate, and pending-approval surfaces all live in one place.

PIs do not have in their nav. The canonical surface for them is the LabPurchases Tool inside the Lab Overview, opened either from a pinned widget tile or from the Tools launcher in the header. The popup is a four-tab dashboard:

A declined purchase carries a timestamp and renders with a red wherever it appears: in the member's own list, in the All Purchases tab, in the Lab Activity stream. The Pending Approvals tab also shows a Recently declined section at the bottom so a PI can Re-approve a previousl...

Click Manage Funding Accounts at the top right and a panel opens inline above the order list. The button label flips to Hide Funding Manager while it is open, so you can collapse it back down without leaving the page. Each account has a name (for example ), a total budget, and...

Each funding account can carry the official grant details beneath its everyday label. Expand Grant details (for data sharing / DOI) on an account and you can record the structured fields a repository deposit needs:

These fields are deliberately separate from the account name. The name is your own short label that purchases match on; the award number is the official identifier and may differ. All of them map one-to-one to the DataCite fields, so when you later deposit an experiment to a r...

A funding account can be linked to a project as its primary grant. That project-to-grant link is what the deposit flow reads when it prefills the funder fields, so a single experiment under that project carries the right award number and funder into its DataCite metadata autom...
A few things in this area are not yet shipped, listed here so you do not spend time looking for them:

Item Name (required): a native autocomplete surfaces every distinct item name from your purchase history, de-duped case-insensitively with the most-recent record winning. When the typed value matches a prior item name exactly, Vendor and Price per unit fill in automatically. Q...
Vendor : free text, optional. Not auto-filled unless an exact Item Name match triggers the recall logic above.

Category : a listing your non-archived, non-shared owned projects plus a synthetic Miscellaneous option at the bottom. Defaults to the first owned project alphabetically; falls back to Miscellaneous if you have no owned projects yet. Choosing Miscellaneous routes the purchase ...
Price per unit and Quantity : numeric free-text inputs. Price defaults blank; quantity defaults to 1.

Funding string : a autocomplete pulls from your existing funding accounts (for example, ). Typing a new string and saving creates a budget-zero funding account automatically so the string appears in future dropdowns without a separate setup step. The field is optional; leave i...

Pending approvals . The work queue. Each row is a purchase waiting on the PI with inline Approve and Decline buttons. The first action of a fresh session unlocks the 5-minute edit window; subsequent rows process without re-prompting. See Edit session and password .
All purchases . A flat lab-wide view of every purchase across every member, filterable by member, project, and funding string.
Funding . The funding-account cards (spent vs. budget) rolled up across the whole lab, not just the PI's own purchases.
Spending . The full spend-over-time chart and the breakdown lenses (project / vendor / category), same shape as the dashboard at the bottom of but with lab-wide scope.
Award number : the official grant identifier (for example ). A soft hint nudges you toward the NIH shape, but any value is accepted.
Funder name : the funding body, with a datalist seeded with NIH and NSF. Choosing NIH auto-fills its funder ID and ID type.
Funder ID and funder ID type : the funder's identifier and its scheme (Crossref Funder ID, ROR, GRID, ISNI, or Other).
Award title : the grant's full title, optional.
Per-item dates (the time axis uses the parent task's start date)
A timestamp
Multi-currency support
Recurring or subscription purchases
OCR receipt import
Anomaly detection and spending alerts
Budget-threshold notifications
A catalog-prefilled vendor and category list (datalist autocomplete is the only source today)

During the onboarding tour, a component mounts on the route to show Alex's fixture data as a read-only overlay. The tour also drives this modal end-to-end via the BeakerBot cursor script. Production users who are not in the tour never see the demo overlay.

Be careful not to conflate two distinct concepts that share the same label. The Category select in NewPurchaseModal picks which project the parent purchase task routes to: choosing "Miscellaneous" routes to the hidden project. Separately, the Miscellaneous filter chip on the o...

The free-text vendor and category columns on individual rows are distinct from the top-level project routing. The top-level routing has one reserved bucket: Miscellaneous routes to the hidden project. One is the project the order lives in, the other is a per-line-item annotati...

When a labmate placed an order on someone else's behalf, advancing that item from Needs ordering to Ordered fires a bell to the requester, so the person who asked for the reagent finds out it is on the way without having to check back. This is the real signal that the order wa...

entries.title: Restriction digest
headings: From a digest to a clone
Split out of the Sequences page
bodySnippets: The restriction digest tools find where enzymes cut your sequence, what overhangs they leave, and what fragment sizes you would see on a gel.

A restriction enzyme recognizes a short, specific sequence of DNA and cuts the double helix at or near that site. Knowing where an enzyme cuts a plasmid, and how many times, is the everyday question behind picking cloning sites, designing a diagnostic digest, and reading a gel...

The digest is a display layer on the open sequence. Turn on the Restriction sites toggle in the editor's view-control rail to overlay cut sites on the circular or linear map, and open the enzyme picker (from the Enzyme toolbar button) to choose which enzymes are active. Nothin...

The Workspace ships with a large catalog of restriction enzymes drawn from a standard NEB-derived dataset (over two hundred enzymes). Each enzyme carries its recognition sequence and its cut geometry, and from that the catalog derives the metadata you filter on: how long the r...

For a given sequence and enzyme set, the digest searches the recognition site on both strands, the forward strand and the reverse complement, so a cut site is found regardless of which way the recognition sequence reads. Each cut records its position and which strand the recog...

When an enzyme cuts, the top-strand cut and the bottom-strand cut may land at the same position or at offset positions. When they coincide, the cut leaves a blunt end. When the top strand cuts before the bottom strand, the cut leaves a 5' overhang; when the top strand cuts aft...

Once the cut positions are known, the digest computes the fragment sizes you would see on a gel, sorted from largest to smallest. Topology matters here. For a linear molecule the two ends are open, so the cuts divide it into fragments with a piece at each end. For a circular m...

The enzyme picker mirrors the SnapGene chooser. You can search enzymes by name, hide enzymes that never cut the open sequence, and restrict the list by cut-count category: only the enzymes that cut exactly once (unique cutters), or those that cut exactly N times, or the non-cu...

Alongside the manual filters, a few computed presets give you a one-click set that is always derived from the current sequence. Common is the everyday workhorse set (EcoRI, BamHI, HindIII, and friends) intersected with the enzymes that actually cut this molecule. Unique cutter...

The restriction digest and the Cloning Workspace are two views of the same enzyme model. Use the digest to find the cutters and overhangs you want, then carry the same enzymes into a restriction-ligation or Golden Gate assembly in the Cloning Workspace to build the construct. ...

This page used to be one section of the Sequences workbench page. The enzyme catalog and digest tools grew enough depth to stand on their own, so they live here now. The digest still runs inside the Sequences editor, on whatever sequence you have open.

Where an enzyme cuts is a question with one correct answer, so the cut detection is not an approximation. The digest is validated band-for-band against Biopython's module, the standard reference for restriction analysis, and that comparison runs as part of the test suite and i...

Cut sites overlaid on the plasmid map once the Enzyme menu's Cut sites toggle is on. Each label points a leader line at the exact cut position, and a count next to an enzyme (XhoI x8) shows how many times it cuts.

A circular plasmid map of pEGFP-N1 with restriction cut sites overlaid as labeled tick marks around the ring (NcoI, XhoI, NotI, SphI and others), each with a leader line to its cut position, shown beside the base-level sequence.

entries.title: Where results live now
headings: Per-project completed work → Workbench Results tab
bodySnippets: ResearchOS used to have a standalone Results page that listed every completed task, grouped by project. That page was retired. Completed work now surfaces in three places, each closer to the context where the work happens.

Experiments that you've marked complete now collect in the Earlier results section at the bottom of the Workbench page, with the header rendered in uppercase as EARLIER RESULTS . They keep all the same write-up affordances: clicking a card opens the task detail popup with the ...
Finished purchase orders appear inline in the Purchases chronological list alongside active orders, marked with a green dot and a text suffix. There is no separate Earlier section.

Clicking a project card on the Home page navigates to the project route at . The Results tab there shows a gallery of result images for that project via the component. This is the fastest way to review all result images for a single project without leaving its context.

Visiting now redirects to Workbench , where the bulk of the old page's content lives. No data moved on disk: every task lives at . New file attachments land in per-tab subdirectories: notes files go to and results files go to , rather than a flat folder at the task root.
is queued for recapture to reflect the current Workbench layout.

The standalone Results page was a fourth surface that mostly duplicated work the per-feature pages were already doing better. Folding completion into Workbench, Purchases, and the project popup keeps each completion view closer to the live work it belongs to, instead of forcin...
The Earlier results section sits at the bottom of the Workbench. The project-grouping toggle in its header flips between flat and project-grouped views.
The Earlier results section at the bottom of the Workbench page, with completed experiment cards grouped by project.

entries.title: Search
headings: What gets searched, and what doesn't
bodySnippets: A filter-driven search page that finds tasks across every project under your user. Fill in the form, click Search, click a result card to open its task popup.

The page opens with an empty filter card and a placeholder that reads "Enter search criteria above." Type into the Keywords box (or use any of the dropdowns), then click the blue Search button. Results render below as a grid of cards, one per matching task. The Clear button ne...
Every filter is optional. Leave them blank to match everything, or combine as many as you want. All filters AND together.

Each card carries a colored stripe matching its project, the task name, a project pill, a task-type pill (purple for experiments, amber for purchases, gray for list tasks), the schedule and duration, the method (when one is attached), and the first few tags. A green Complete b...

The Search page doubles as the launch pad for bulk exports. Click the Select button next to the result count and the cards sprout a checkbox in the top-right corner. Tick one or more result cards (a blue ring and the checked mark show selection), and the header swaps in a coun...

Clicking Export selected opens the same Export dialog the experiment popup uses, with three format cards: PDF report , HTML report , and Raw ResearchOS format . The dialog heading reads Export N experiments with a reminder that multi-experiment exports produce a zip with one f...
See Export an experiment on the Experiments page for a deeper walkthrough of what each format bundles and which one to pick.
Keywords matches the task name, its tags, and the name and tags of its first attached method. Multiple words AND together (e.g., only returns tasks where both words appear).
Date From and Date To restrict to tasks whose schedule overlaps the range.
Task Type narrows to one of the three task shapes (i.e., Experiments, Purchases, or List Tasks).
Project limits results to tasks inside one project.
Specific Method matches tasks whose first attached method is the one you pick. Method Category is the broader version: it matches on the method's folder.
Completion Status filters to complete or incomplete tasks.
PDF packages each experiment as its own selectable-text PDF with table of contents and outline pane, then drops every PDF into a single .
HTML gives each experiment its own subfolder inside the zip (one self-contained HTML page plus an tree), so you can open each report independently without an extra unzip step.
Raw nests one per experiment inside the outer zip, keeping each bundle ready to drop into another ResearchOS user's Settings → Import experiment dialog.
Pressing Enter while focused on the Keywords box runs the search without needing to click the button.

Keywords match against task names, task tags, and method names and tags; they do not match against note bodies, results text, or PCR protocol contents. Open the Notes page or a method's own editor to search the inside of those files.
The Search page. Type into Keywords, narrow with the filters, then click Search.
The Search page with a keywords input, a row of filter dropdowns, and Search and Clear buttons.
Select mode on Search. Tick the cards you want and click Export selected to open the format dialog.
Search results in select mode: three cards ticked with blue rings and the Export selected button visible at the top of the results list.

entries.title: Sequences
headings: The tab bar and view modes
Connection to the rest of the app
bodySnippets: The Sequences workbench is where plasmids, oligos, genes, and protein sequences live alongside your experiments and notes.

The Sequences workbench is a SnapGene-style sequence editor built directly into ResearchOS. It holds every DNA, RNA, and protein sequence in your lab folder in one indexed library, and it opens those sequences in a rich editor that renders circular plasmid maps, annotated line...

The central design choice is that your sequences are first-class records alongside your experiments and notes, not attachments tucked inside a separate application. A plasmid you designed last month, the oligos for a current cloning reaction, the protein from a collaborator's ...

The left panel of the workbench is the library. It lists every sequence in your folder, grouped into collections that map to your projects. A collection selector at the top of the panel lets you narrow the list to one project or view everything at once. Within a collection, se...

Each library row shows the sequence name, the sequence type (DNA, RNA, or protein), the length in base pairs or amino acids, and the topology for DNA sequences (circular or linear). Clicking a row opens the sequence in the editor. A small button at the top of the library panel...

There are three ways to add a sequence. The New Sequence dialog creates a sequence from scratch: you name it, pick a type and topology, and type or paste the bases directly. The Import path accepts SnapGene files (.dna, .prot), GenBank (.gb, .gbk), FASTA (.fa, .fasta, includin...

Sequences produced by the Cloning Workspace land in the library automatically when you save the cloning product. Primers you design in the editor are saved as features on the sequence, not as separate library entries, so the oligo stays with the template it belongs to.

The bottom of the editor panel carries a tab bar with five tabs: Map, Sequence, Features, Primers, and History. These tabs are the primary view switcher for the open sequence, modeled on SnapGene's bottom navigation. The Features and Primers tabs carry a count badge showing ho...

The Map tab renders the sequence as a circular plasmid map or a linear map, depending on the sequence topology and the topology toggle in the view-control rail. The Sequence tab renders the bases at nucleotide resolution with the complement strand, translation tracks, and rule...

A narrow vertical strip of icon buttons runs along the left edge of the editor. This is the view-control rail. Each button is a toggle: pressing it switches a display layer on or off without changing the active tab. The rail carries toggles for features, restriction enzyme cut...

The Features toggle has a secondary disclosure caret below it. Clicking the caret opens a small flyout listing every feature type present on the sequence, with an eye icon per type so you can hide specific annotation categories (promoters, for example) without hiding everythin...

When the Map tab is active, plasmid sequences render as a circular map: a color-coded ring where each annotated feature appears as a named arc in the color assigned to its feature type. Clicking a feature arc on the map selects it and scrolls the Features panel to that entry. ...

The Sequence tab renders the sequence at nucleotide resolution. The forward strand runs left to right; a complement strand renders below it; feature and primer annotations appear as labeled colored tracks above or below the bases; and a coordinate ruler above the bases shows p...

The editor is editable by default. Typing at the cursor inserts bases; backspace and delete remove them; and selecting a range and typing replaces it. The keyboard shortcuts for copy, cut, paste, select-all, undo, and redo all work as they do in a text editor. Cut and paste ca...

A toolbar above the editor carries the Undo, Redo, and Save buttons, plus dropdown menus for Edit operations, Feature and Primer actions, Enzyme management, and Analyze tools. Saving is explicit: the Save button records a new checkpoint in the sequence history. The History tab...

A Find box in the toolbar searches by DNA sequence (exact match, with a closest-match fallback for near-misses), by feature name, or by protein sequence. Each match highlights in the viewer and the arrow keys step through multiple hits.

When you click or drag to select bases in the editor, a readout bar at the bottom of the editor updates immediately with the selection coordinates, the length in base pairs, and the GC content as a percentage. For selections between 8 and 50 bp the readout also shows the predi...

Features are the annotation layer of a sequence. Each feature has a name, a type (CDS, promoter, primer_bind, rep_origin, and so on), a strand, a color, a set of coordinates, and an optional set of GenBank qualifiers (/product, /gene, /note, and others). The Sequences workbenc...

The most direct path is to select a region on the map or sequence and open the feature editor from the context menu or the Feature toolbar menu. The feature editor dialog mirrors the SnapGene "Edit Feature" interface: name, type, strand, a segment table for multi-segment featu...

For sequences without annotations, two automated paths under the Analyze menu can propose features. Detect Features runs a local database scan against bundled protein and DNA element reference sets: it identifies common elements such as fluorescent proteins, origins of replica...
The Cloning Workspace is a multi-fragment assembly tool launched from the Assemble button in the library header. It supports four assembly chemistries from one method-picker interface.

Overlap assembly (Gibson / NEBuilder HiFi) takes two or more fragments from the library or pasted directly, designs the junction overlap regions to a target length or Tm, and assembles them into a linear or circular product. The junction primers needed to amplify each fragment...

Restriction and ligation cuts two sequences at chosen restriction enzyme sites (from a set of common cutters: EcoRI, BamHI, HindIII, PstI, KpnI, SmaI, XhoI, NotI) and ligates the compatible ends, computing all possible directional products and letting you pick the intended one.

Golden Gate (Type IIS) uses a chosen Type IIS enzyme (BsaI, BsmBI, BbsI, or SapI) to assemble fragments via non-palindromic four-base overhangs, producing a scarless product at each junction. Gateway (BP or LR) simulates the recombination-based transfer between entry and desti...

All four chemistries share the same two-step flow: pick fragments and set options, then review the computed product. The review screen shows the assembled sequence with its features and any warnings (such as internal cut sites that would be severed by the chosen enzyme). Savin...

The primer tools in the Sequences workbench are built around the binding site, not just the sequence. The Add Primer dialog (accessible from the Primer toolbar menu or by selecting a region and using the context menu) takes a primer sequence by typing, pasting, or seeding from...

Double-clicking an existing primer_bind feature on the map or sequence opens the Edit Primer dialog, which shows the same stats and alignment but seeds everything from the stored feature. Editing the oligo in this dialog re-derives the binding site and Tm live; saving writes b...

The Primer Design mode in the Add Primer dialog includes a mutagenesis path: opening the dialog in mutagenesis mode lets you specify a substitution, insertion, or deletion at a chosen position, and the engine designs a primer with the mutation incorporated and the flanking bas...

After designing a primer, a specificity check against NCBI BLAST is available from the Primers panel. Clicking the BLAST link for a primer opens the NCBI Primer-BLAST interface in a new tab with the primer sequence pre-filled, handing off to NCBI's servers to check for off-tar...

The Compare / Align dialog (accessible from the Analyze menu in the editor or from the library header) accepts two sequences from the library and runs a pairwise alignment. Two alignment modes are available: global (Needleman-Wunsch, end-to-end alignment) and local (Smith-Wate...

The result shows the percent identity, the number of mismatches and gaps, and the full wrapped alignment with a match/mismatch midline and coordinate ticks. For shorter sequences a k-mer dotplot renders below the alignment, giving a visual read on repeat structure or structura...

Site-directed mutagenesis primer design is available directly from the sequence editor. Select the target region, open the Add Primer dialog from the Primer menu, and switch to Mutagenesis mode. The mutagenesis designer accepts three mutation types: substitution (change one or...

Every explicit Save in the sequence editor records a checkpoint in the sequence history. The History tab shows these checkpoints as a timeline, with the date and time of each save. Selecting a checkpoint shows the sequence state at that point and the diff between that checkpoi...

Sequences can be exported in GenBank format or FASTA format from the Export menu in the editor toolbar. The GenBank export preserves all features, qualifiers, and topology flags in the standard .gb format that any sequence analysis tool can read. The FASTA export writes the ra...

Sequences participate in the shared folder structure alongside notes, experiments, and methods. A sequence is filed under a project through the collection it belongs to, so it shows up in the same project context as the experiments that use it. Deleting a sequence goes through...

The primer Tm engine in the Sequences workbench is the same nearest-neighbor model used elsewhere in the app. The same nearest-neighbor Tm model powers the Primer Tm tab in the Lab calculators modal, so a Tm you compute from a selection in the sequence editor and one you compu...

Deleting a sequence moves it to the Trash with a 30-day recovery window, the same as notes and tasks. The sequence is removed from the library immediately, but it is recoverable until the window expires.

Enable the Restriction sites toggle in the view-control rail to overlay cut sites on the map. The default set of common restriction enzymes is pre-loaded; the enzyme picker (accessible from the Enzyme toolbar button) lets you filter to any subset.

The Tm displayed in the selection readout is the same nearest-neighbor model that powers the Primer Tm tab in the Lab calculators modal, using SantaLucia parameters. Selecting the exact span of a primer you are designing is a quick way to sanity-check the Tm before adding it.

Sequence history uses the same underlying engine as the Version History system for notes. The checkpoint model and restore path are shared; the sequence surface just exposes them through the editor's History tab rather than the per-note timeline panel.

PCR protocol templates in the Methods Library store primer sequences and annealing temperatures as part of the protocol record. If you design primers in the Sequences workbench and then attach the protocol to an experiment, the primer fields in the protocol and the primer_bind...
The Sequences workbench: the library on the left, the editor and map on the right.
The Sequences workbench showing the library panel on the left and the editor surface on the right, with a circular plasmid map rendered in the main view area.
The collection selector scopes the library to one project or opens it to everything at once.
The library panel with the collection selector open, showing a list of project names and an All collections option.
The view-control rail. Active layers show a sky-blue background; inactive layers are gray. A small caret under the Features toggle opens the per-type show/hide flyout.
The view-control rail showing a column of small icon toggle buttons along the left edge of the editor, with the Features and Ruler toggles lit in sky blue.
A circular plasmid map. Feature arcs are color-coded by type. Click any arc to select the feature.
A circular plasmid map with labeled feature arcs in several colors (blue CDS arcs, green promoter arcs, orange rep_origin arc) and a position ring with tick marks.
The Sequence view at base-character resolution. The ruler shows 1-based positions; feature tracks sit above the forward strand.
The Sequence tab at high zoom, showing individual base characters in monospace with the complement strand beneath, feature arcs above, and the coordinate ruler at the top.
The selection readout updates live as you drag. Tm appears for oligo-length selections (8-50 bp).
The selection readout bar at the bottom of the editor showing the range coordinates, length in bp, GC percent, and a Tm value for a short selected region.
The feature editor. Qualifiers follow the GenBank standard; add or remove rows freely.
The feature editor dialog open over the sequence editor, showing name, type, strand, and color fields along with a segment table and qualifiers section.
The Cloning Workspace. Pick a chemistry from the four method pills, then add fragments from the library or paste them in.
The Cloning Workspace showing the four method pills (Overlap, Restriction, Golden Gate, Gateway) with the Overlap method selected and a fragment picker below.
The review step. Warnings flag potential problems (internal cut sites, mismatched overhangs). Accept or go back to adjust.
The Cloning Workspace review step showing the assembled product with its features, junction warnings in amber, and a Save to library button.
The Add Primer dialog. Stats update live as you type. The alignment shows the annealing region and any 5' tail.

entries.title: Settings
headings: What a password protects, and what it doesn't
bodySnippets: Fourteen panels stacked top to bottom: Profile, Tabs, LabArchives, AI Helper, Sidebar, View defaults, Animation, Notifications & behavior, Streaks, Data inventory, Data maintenance, Onboarding, Security, and Offline mode. Reach the page through the gear icon in the top-right o...

Each change saves the moment you toggle, type, or pick a new option. A small "Saving..." pill near the page title flips to a green "Saved" for about a second, then disappears. The path printed under the page title shows where your preferences are stored on disk ( ), which is h...

At the very top of the page, above every panel, a search bar filters the visible settings down to whatever matches your typed substring. Type and the page collapses to the Security panel; type and it narrows to the Animation section. The filter matches section headings and the...

PI accounts ( ) see a two-tab strip at the top of the page: Personal (the default, every panel documented below) and Lab Mode (the lab-wide configuration that only a PI can change). The Personal tab is identical to what every account sees. The Lab Mode tab holds the LabRoster ...

The top panel sets how the rest of the app addresses you. A large avatar bubble on the left previews the gradient that'll appear in comments, lab views, and the user-picker. The Display name field overrides your folder name (leave it blank to fall back to the folder name).

The Primary color row is a fixed 10-color palette. Clicking a swatch updates the avatar preview instantly and refreshes every avatar bubble across the app on the next paint. A swatch is grayed out when another lab member already holds that color as a solid (direction-insensiti...

Below the primary row sits an Optional second color for gradient row. Pick any swatch that is not your primary and is not already taken by a lab-mate to create a two-stop gradient. The avatar preview updates immediately. A Clear secondary button appears next to the row label w...
The Tint header with my color toggle controls one thing: whether the top header bar uses your gradient or stays plain white. Your avatar bubbles keep your color either way.

A two-column grid of checkboxes for every tab in the header (Home, Workbench, GANTT, Methods, Purchases, Calendar, Search, Lab Links). Uncheck a tab to hide it from your header. Home is grayed out with an "always on" tag so you can't accidentally hide every tab at once.

Below the grid, the Default landing tab dropdown picks where ResearchOS opens when you load the app. The dropdown only lists tabs you have visible (plus Home), so you can't land somewhere you've hidden.

A single option card for bulk-importing a LabArchives Offline Notebook ZIP into ResearchOS. The card has a title, a short description, and a small button that expands an explainer about why the existing users can opt back in here any time.
Clicking Re-run tour clears the tour's completion and skip flags so Phase 1 setup runs again, then launches the in-product walkthrough immediately without a page reload.

If a previous tour left demo data on your real account (because the auto-cleanup at the end was skipped), an amber recovery banner appears above the button. It shows the count of leftover demo items and explains that re-running the tour will offer to clean them up at the end.

A single panel for managing the password gate on this account. The line of text in the panel shows whether the password is currently set (green) or not set (gray). The Set password or Change password button opens the same Account password popup that the lock icon on the user-p...

In the popup you can set a new password (minimum 4 characters), change an existing one (current password required), or remove the password entirely. Passwords are hashed with PBKDF2-SHA-256 at 600k iterations before being written to disk.

A single toggle, Block calls to our server , that disables the two proxy routes the app uses for external data fetching: (ICS calendar sync) and (Telegram CDN file downloads). Enabling this makes the app make no outbound calls to its own server.

Direct Telegram polling (browser to ) continues regardless of this setting, because that path does not go through the app's proxy. An amber notice appears while the toggle is on, confirming which features are paused.
Settings doesn't appear in the tabs grid. It sits behind the gear icon in the header so it can never be hidden. Even if you uncheck every other tab, the gear icon and Home keep working.

The password gate stops a lab member from accidentally signing into the wrong account through the app UI and editing someone else's notes. It does not encrypt your files. Anyone with access to the shared folder (OneDrive, Dropbox, iCloud) can still read your raw markdown and i...

Since ResearchOS has no server, there's no "reset link" email. To clear a forgotten password, open the shared data folder in Finder or Explorer, go into , and delete . Sign in again and the gate is gone. A lab admin (or anyone with access to the folder) can do this for you. Th...

Every user's preferences are stored at inside the connected data folder. Switching users in the picker loads that user's settings. Switching to a different folder gives you a clean slate of defaults until you tweak it.

The Settings page showing its stack of panels: Profile, Tabs, LabArchives, AI Helper, Sidebar, View defaults, Animation, Notifications & behavior, Streaks, Data inventory, Data maintenance, Onboarding, Security, and Offline mode.

entries.title: Sharing and permissions
headings: The two primitives: canRead and canWrite
Hosting a labmate's task in your project
Older records with permission: view | edit
bodySnippets: Every shareable record in ResearchOS (a method, a note, a task, a project, a high-level goal) carries one sharing field: shared_with, an array of small objects pairing a username with a permission level. That field plus a single sentinel value for 'the whole lab' is the entire...
Every shareable record carries a field whose TypeScript signature is:

Each entry pairs a recipient with the level of access they get on this record. An empty array means "private to me": the owner is implicit and never appears in their own list. Adding an entry grants that user the chosen level. Removing the entry revokes access entirely. The sh...
Inspecting your JSON folder, you will see the objects on disk. A method shared with two members at different levels looks like:

A reserved username, the constant with the value , can appear in any entry where a real username would. It means "every user in this lab folder, present and future." When you flip the Share with the whole lab toggle in the In your lab tab of any share dialog, the affordance wr...

The sentinel is comparable to a Unix group: one entry that expands to the current member set at read time. The backing list is not stored, so adding or archiving a lab member never requires a sweep across every record to keep things in sync.
Every permission decision in the app routes through one of two pure, synchronous functions:
Both functions are pure: same input, same output, no I/O. They are synchronous, so every render and every save can call them without async ceremony.

A user whose gets an extra rule on the read side only: returns true for every record in the lab regardless of . The Lab Overview cross-lab dashboards depend on this rule: the member workload widget has to be able to read every member's active tasks even when those tasks are pr...

The implicit view-all does not extend to writes. A PI reading a member's private note can leave a comment (comments respect read access) but cannot edit the note body unless the lab-head edit-session is unlocked for that member's data. See PI for the broader role and edit sess...

One non-obvious rule sits next to : when a user shares a task with you, you also get transient read access on any method that task references. The pure helper in performs the depth-1 check, and the read path emits a entry into the method owner's audit log so they can see who h...
Sharing is per-record:

Sharing is not silent on the receiving end. When a labmate adds you to a record, it shows up in your Inbox under the Shared with me segment, the running list of everything people in your folder have shared to you, newest first. The Inbox badge carries a count so you can tell a...

Sharing a task one way is read or edit access on that task. Hosting goes a step further: a labmate can let one of their tasks also appear inside your project , so the experiment lives in your project board alongside your own tasks without ever leaving its original owner's fold...

The link is bidirectional : the task points at your project and your project points back at the task, and both sides have to agree for the host to count. If the two ever drift apart (a task gets deleted, or the reference goes stale), ResearchOS self-heals the mismatch on read ...
Pages elsewhere in the wiki that touch sharing decisions:
canRead(record, viewer) : true if the viewer is the owner, OR the viewer's account_type is (implicit view-all), OR has an entry whose matches the viewer OR is .

canWrite(record, viewer, session) : true if the viewer is the owner, OR the viewer is a PI AND the edit-session is currently unlocked for the record's owner (the Phase 5 passcode flow, see PI edit session ), OR has an entry whose matches (or is ) AND the entry's is .

Methods can be shared with the whole lab or with specific users. Public-sharing a method makes it readable across libraries; the structured-method editor still gates writes on , which usually keeps edits to the original creator. See Methods Library .

Projects share the project itself plus every task under it. The dialog offers view-only or edit permission. The owner's Home page is the source of truth; the receiver sees a live read of the owner's data.
Tasks can be shared one at a time when you want a single experiment visible without sharing the whole project.
Notes can be shared the same way.
Methods Library covers method-level sharing UI.
Home and Projects covers project-level sharing and the receiver's view.
The Workbench covers task-level sharing.
PI covers the implicit view-all rule.
Notifications covers the Inbox, the Shared with me segment, and the badge that flags a new share.
Security covers what the gate actually protects against (and what it does not).

Pre-R1 records (and older share-API callers) used a field with values or instead of . The reader ( in ) accepts both shapes so the rest of the app only ever sees the unified field ( maps to ; unknown values fall back to , the conservative default). Records still on the legacy ...

ResearchOS used to ship with a special "Lab Mode" account that held shared records on behalf of the whole lab. That mode has been retired in favor of per-user accounts plus the sharing primitive described above; pre-retirement folders auto-migrate on first login. Any old recor...
The In your lab tab of the share dialog. Each recipient has its own read or edit toggle, and a Share with the whole lab toggle covers present and future members at read-only by default.
The In your lab tab of the share dialog over a method popup, showing recipient rows each with a read/edit toggle, plus a Share with the whole lab toggle below.

entries.title: Trash & History
headings: What R2 does not yet do
Deleted vs edited: two different safety nets
Bulk restore skips the parent prompt
bodySnippets: A recovery window for deletes. When you delete a note, task, project, method, purchase, goal, lab link, mass spec protocol, or sequence, it does not vanish. It moves into a per-user trash folder where it sits for 30 days. Within that window you can restore it back to its origi...

Research data has a different relationship to time than consumer data. A note about a PCR run from eight months ago is not "old, who cares," it is the experimental record. A misclick on the delete button used to be irrecoverable; the new trash flow gives you a safety net witho...

When you delete a note, the file moves from into . A small metadata block is added to the trashed record that records when the delete happened, who issued it, and when the auto-cleanup sweep will permanently remove it.

A sidecar index at keeps a flat summary of every trashed record. The index is rebuilt automatically from the directory listing if it ever goes out of sync (manual file deletion, OneDrive merge conflict, partial crash). The on-disk files are the ground truth; the index is just ...

Open the trash from the small trash-can icon in the top-right corner of the header (next to the Settings gear) or from the Settings -> History & Trash section. Trashed records are grouped into sections by what they are, so all your deleted notes sit together, all your deleted ...
Each row shows:

A sort dropdown in the header switches between Newest first (default), Oldest first, and Expiring soon (urgent-cleanup-first). The sort applies inside each section, so the most relevant rows float to the top of every category at once.

Cleaning up after a big experiment usually means dealing with more than one stray record at a time. Rather than restoring or deleting rows one by one, you can select several and act on them in a single pass. The checkbox on each row drives the selection, and the checkbox on ea...

Defaults to 30 days . Change it under Settings -> History & Trash . Four options: 7 days, 30 days, 90 days, or Never. The Never option means automatic cleanup never fires; you can still delete from the trash page manually.

The cleanup pass runs once on every folder-connect. If you keep ResearchOS open for weeks at a time, the sweep waits until your next cold start to clear expired entries. Multi-device users get the same behavior on each device when they connect their folder.
Only the record owner sees the Delete button. If a labmate has shared edit access to one of your notes, they can edit it, but they cannot delete it; that's your call as the owner.

The one carve-out is the PI lab-head edit session. When a PI is in an active Phase 5 unlock , they can delete records owned by other lab members. The trash entry records the PI as the deleter and ties the entry to the unlock session so the audit log groups consistently with th...
Click Restore on the row. The record is written back to its original path with the metadata block stripped off, and the trash file is removed. The record is now live again at the same id.
When restoring a record whose parent (e.g. a Project that contains the task) is also in trash, a prompt asks whether to restore both or just the child. See "Restoring with a trashed parent" below.

Use Permanent delete on the row to remove a trash entry ahead of the cleanup window. The button asks for confirmation and is final. After permanent delete, the record is gone; there is no second-level recycle bin.
Projects are the one entity type with TWO recovery states. Both coexist by design:

Records like Tasks (parent: Project), Notes (no parent), Purchase Items (parent: Task), and High-level Goals (parent: Project) record a soft reference to their parent at delete time. When you restore a record whose parent is ALSO in trash, a prompt asks:
If the parent is not in trash (the common case), no prompt fires and the restore proceeds straight through.

Per-record edit history (the per-save timeline plus a revert button) is now shipping on a separate track. It is live today on free-form Notes as a rolling-out pilot; see Version History for what is on and where it goes next. The settings panel also surfaces an "Orphaned files"...
A checkbox for selecting the row (more on that below).
The record's name (recovered from the original title).
Who deleted it and when.
A countdown until auto-cleanup ("Expires in 27 days").
A Restore button (returns the record to its original location) and a Permanent delete button (removes the trash file immediately, with a confirmation step).

Archive (the existing button): the project stays at with . It is hidden from default views but visible in the archived list. Use this for projects you are done with but want to keep on disk for reference.

Delete (now routes through trash): the project file moves to and the cleanup window starts. The trash entry preserves the flag, so a project you archived first and then deleted will come back archived on restore.
Restore both (default): restores the parent first, then this record.
Just this record : leaves the parent in trash. The record will exist but its parent reference will dangle.
Cancel : nothing happens.
Pick your rows. Tick the checkbox on any row you want to act on. Selections can span more than one section, so you can grab two notes and a task in the same go.

Or grab a whole category. The checkbox in a section header selects every row in that section at once. When only some of the rows in a section are selected, the header checkbox shows a dash to tell you the section is partly selected rather than fully selected.

Act from the bar. As soon as something is selected, a bar slides in at the top of the list showing how many items are selected. From there you can Restore all of them, Permanent delete all of them, or Clear selection to start over.
Confirm a bulk delete. Restoring in bulk happens right away. A bulk permanent delete is irreversible, so it asks you to confirm in a dialog first, and only then removes the selected records for good.

Trash recovers a record you deleted . It does not help with a record you edited : a paragraph you overwrote three saves ago is not in the trash, because the note itself was never deleted. For that, ResearchOS keeps a separate per-save timeline you can scroll back through and (...

If a row leaves the trash while it is selected, whether you restored it on its own or the list reloaded, it quietly drops out of the selection. The count in the bar always reflects what is actually still sitting in the trash.

Restoring a single record asks what to do when its parent is also in the trash (see "Restoring with a trashed parent" below). A bulk restore does not stop to ask. Each selected record is restored on its own, so if you want a parent and child to come back together, select both ...
Each user's cleanup window applies to their own trash. If a PI deletes one of your records during an unlock session, the trash entry lands in your folder and inherits your cleanup setting.
You can archive a project today and trash it next month. Restoring from trash strips the trash metadata only; the archive state survives the round trip.

Every delete you make through the app is recoverable for at least seven days (the most aggressive cleanup setting). Within the default 30-day window, a misclick is a one-button fix. We do not promise the trash is tamper-proof; the files sit in your folder and you can edit them...
Selecting rows reveals a bar with Restore, Permanent delete, and Clear selection.
Trash page with two rows selected and the bulk action bar showing Restore, Permanent delete, and Clear selection

entries.title: Version History
headings: Grouped by day, then by session
Who sees it: local and private
Color is never the only signal
bodySnippets: Every time you save a note, ResearchOS quietly records what changed, when, and who changed it. Open the version history sidebar and you can scroll back through that timeline, see each edit highlighted in place, and roll the note back to any earlier state. It is the experimenta...

A version is a single saved state of a note. You do not create versions by hand and there is no "commit" button to remember. The editor already decides when to flush your work to disk (see Saving in the editor ), and every one of those saves appends one row to an append-only h...

Because the file is append-only and stores deltas, the history never rewrites the live note. The note you are editing is always the ground truth; the history is a side-channel that the save path writes after the note is safely on disk, and a failure to write history can never ...

This is a different kind of safety net than the Trash . Trash recovers a note you deleted ; version history recovers a note you edited . A paragraph you overwrote three saves ago is not in the trash, because the note itself was never deleted, but it is sitting in the version h...

Open any note and look for the clock-arrow history control on the note popup. Click it and a version history sidebar slides in on the right while the note body stays on the left. The sidebar is the timeline; the body column becomes the place where each version is shown.

Each row in the sidebar is one version. It shows the editor's avatar, a one-line summary of what that save changed (for example, "edited 2 paragraphs"), and a relative timestamp like "3 hours ago." Hover the timestamp for the full date and time. The newest state sits at the to...

A working note can accumulate dozens of saves in an afternoon. A flat list of every save would be noise, so the sidebar groups them the way you actually remember your work: by day first, then by editing session within the day.

Select any version and the note body column does not just show that old snapshot. It shows a diff : the note as it stands, with the selected version's changes marked inline. Added lines render in green, removed lines in red with a strike-through, and unchanged prose renders no...

On a note that more than one person edits, the diff goes a step further and tints each change by editor . Every changed run of lines carries a left border in that editor's personal color, with their avatar at the start of the run, so you can see at a glance that Mira rewrote t...

By default, a version is diffed against the one immediately before it, so you see exactly what that single save changed. A toggle at the top of the sidebar switches the comparison base to the current version instead, which answers a different question: "what is different betwe...

Version history is part of your data folder, not a cloud service. The history file sits inside the note owner's folder on disk, right beside the note, and it travels with the rest of your data through whatever sync you already use (OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, and the othe...

Visibility follows the same rules as the note itself. A note that is private to you keeps its history private to you; if you share the note with the lab, a labmate who can read the note can read its history too. The history does not widen access, and it does not leak past edit...

Selecting any earlier version reveals a Restore this version button in a sticky footer at the bottom of the sidebar (the current version has nothing to restore to, so it never shows the button). Clicking it asks for an inline confirmation rather than popping a browser dialog, ...

Restoring does not erase anything. The note's current state is kept as a version in the history before the older state becomes the live note, so a restore is just one more forward edit on the timeline. You can always see what the note looked like before you restored it.

A restore is reversible. For 24 hours after you restore a version, an Undo restore affordance lets you put the note back the way it was before the restore, in one click. After the window closes the restore simply stands as a normal edit in the history, which you can still walk...

A per-entry edit history with the ability to revert is one of the things funders and reviewers look for in a research record: it is what makes the record provenanced , a tamper-evident trail of who changed what and when. ResearchOS records that trail automatically and keeps it...
Day headers ("Today," "Yesterday," then dated) split the timeline into the days you worked on the note.

Sessions bundle a run of consecutive saves by the same editor into one collapsible group. A burst of ten small saves while you tightened up a protocol collapses to a single "Mira, 10 versions" row you can expand when you need the detail and leave folded when you do not.

Version history, including restore and the 24-hour undo, is on for everyone on free-form Notes , Tasks , and Projects . Nothing to turn on. The same timeline, diff, and restore also power the Sequences history tab . Results and methods are still on the roadmap. The page below ...

Notes with a very long history are paginated (a Load older versions button at the bottom), and saves from far enough back are summarized into a single "earlier versions" note rather than kept row by row. You always keep the recent, detailed timeline; the deep past is condensed...

Added and removed lines always carry the green/red coloring, the strike-through, and a / gutter mark in addition to the editor tint, so the diff reads clearly for color-blind users. The per-editor color is supplementary, never load-bearing on its own.
The version history sidebar. Saves are grouped by day, then by editing session, with the current version pinned at the top.

A note open with the version history sidebar on the right, showing versions grouped under day headers and editing sessions, each row with an editor avatar, a one-line change summary, and a relative timestamp.
The selected version's changes, shown in place. Each changed run carries a left border tinted with the editor's color and their avatar, so who-changed-what reads at a glance.
A note body showing an in-place diff: added lines in green, removed lines in red with strike-through, with a colored left border and an editor avatar marking the run of lines a given person changed.
The compare base toggle. Previous shows what one save changed; Current shows the full delta from an old state to today.
The compare toggle at the top of the version history sidebar, a two-option segmented control reading Previous and Current, with Previous selected.
An earlier version offers a Restore button. The confirm step is inline, not a native dialog.
The version history sidebar with an earlier version selected and a green Restore this version button in a sticky footer, plus the inline confirm and cancel prompt.
For 24 hours after a restore, Undo restore reverses it in one click. The undo is itself recorded as a forward edit.
The note popup header showing an Undo restore button after a restore, with a note that the undo window is open for 24 hours.

entries.title: Getting Started
bodySnippets: Short pages that take you from a brand-new visitor to a working ResearchOS install, or skip ahead to the in-browser demo.
Work through these in order:

entries.title: Browser Requirements
headings: Keep Firefox or Safari as your daily driver
bodySnippets: ResearchOS reads and writes files directly on your computer, which only some browsers can do.
Google Chrome (version 86+) on Windows, macOS, Linux, ChromeOS.
Microsoft Edge (version 86+) on Windows, macOS.
Other Chromium-based browsers (e.g., Arc, Vivaldi, Opera) usually work but aren't officially tested.

Brave is built on Chromium but deliberately removes the File System Access API for privacy reasons, and there is no reliable way to turn it back on. ResearchOS can't open your folder in Brave. Use Chrome or Edge instead.
Firefox doesn't implement the File System Access API. Your data is still stored in inside your folder, so once Firefox ships support, your existing data will load.
Safari (macOS / iOS) has the same limitation as Firefox.
Mobile browsers (e.g., Chrome / Edge / Brave on iOS or Android) won't work. The API is desktop-only.
Chrome : google.com/chrome
Edge : microsoft.com/edge (preinstalled on Windows 10/11)

ResearchOS uses the File System Access API to read and write JSON files directly to a folder you pick. This is what lets the app run with no server, no account, and no upload of your data. While Firefox and Safari haven't shipped support yet, those browsers can't open Research...
You only need a Chromium browser open while you're using ResearchOS . Many users keep Firefox or Safari for everyday browsing and open Chrome only for ResearchOS.

entries.title: Connecting Your Folder
headings: Why there is one card, not two
The page layout, at a glance
Starting fresh? Make an empty folder first
What gets created inside the folder
Setting up a shared lab folder
The walkthrough is optional and doesn't link a folder
If Chrome refuses the folder you picked
Already have data? Pick the same folder.
bodySnippets: Pick a folder on your disk. ResearchOS will read and write JSON files inside it (no cloud, no upload). On a brand-new visit, BeakerBot waves at you from the upper-right with an optional 3-minute walkthrough you can take before linking anything.

ResearchOS works against a real folder on your disk, and Chrome's File System Access API can only ever open a folder you point it at. It cannot create a folder from the picker dialog (the OS picker blocks the parent locations a new-folder flow would need, even the Documents ro...
Three things are on this screen on a first visit:

Below the card sit an Explore demo in browser button (it opens a seeded fake yeast lab at so you can look around without linking anything) and a smaller Or download as a starter folder link for the same fake dataset as a real folder you can link.

Because the picker can only open a folder that already exists, the card spells out a short make-it-yourself recipe under the heading Starting fresh? Make an empty folder first: . Do this with your normal file manager (Finder on Mac, Explorer on Windows) before you click Link F...

The Take the 3-minute walkthrough button on the picker opens a small 4-beat modal that introduces the app before you commit to picking a folder. It is opt-in, not auto-fire: brand-new users see the speech bubble's gentle nudge ("New here? It is strongly recommended to take a s...

A small Skip link sits in the corner of every beat so you can bail back to the picker at any time. The modal does NOT write anything to disk and does NOT persist a "seen" flag; reopening it is a one-click decision you make each visit.
ResearchOS creates a simple tree the first time you connect:
Everything is plain JSON and plain image files. You can back up the folder by copying it, version-control it with git, or open it in Finder / Explorer at any time.

After your first connect, ResearchOS remembers the folder name via browser storage. On your next visit, instead of the Link a folder card you see a dedicated reconnect screen : it shows the folder name in a bold heading ( Reconnect to [your-folder] ) and a prominent Continue b...

If multiple people in your lab should share one folder, put the folder inside OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, or iCloud, and follow Shared Lab Accounts . The critical step is making sure the folder is always available offline on every member's laptop.

The Link a folder card in the center : one card, titled Link a folder . It holds a short description, the "Starting fresh? Make an empty folder first" instructions, a drag-and-drop zone, and the Link Folder button. This is the whole folder flow, link an existing folder or a br...

BeakerBot in the upper-right : a small sky-blue beaker mascot waving at you. Below the mascot is a white speech bubble that explains the optional walkthrough, plus a button labeled Take the 3-minute walkthrough . Skip past it entirely if you already know what you want to do.
The RISE credentials stamp in the bottom-right : a small badge that names the academic project this app was built under (a UW-Madison RISE grant). Pure authority signal; nothing to click.
Welcome. BeakerBot waves and gives you a two-sentence pitch for ResearchOS. There is also a small heart easter egg if you click the mascot.
Data security. The core promise: your data NEVER leaves your computer. No upload, no central server, no telemetry on your research. See Security for the full story.
Folder choice. Local (recommended for solo) or cloud-synced (for cross-device or multi-person lab). Local skips the next beat and closes the modal; cloud advances to beat 4.

Cloud provider. Picks the cloud you want to host the folder in (OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, Box, iCloud) and links you to the per-provider setup guide. Closing the modal returns you to the picker; the folder link itself still happens through the Link a folder card.

Open your file manager and make a new folder anywhere you like (Documents/ResearchOS works well). IMPORTANT : Chrome blocks the Desktop, Documents, and Downloads folders themselves, but a folder you make inside any of them works fine.
Name it something like .
Click Link Folder below the recipe and select the folder you just made, not its top-level parent.

Link the folder. You have two equivalent ways: click the Link Folder button to open your operating system's folder picker, or drag a folder directly onto the Link a folder card . The card highlights with a dashed blue border, and its hint text changes from "Drop your folder he...
The browser asks for permission to read and write that folder. Click Allow on every visit so you don't get reprompted.

ResearchOS initializes the folder structure (e.g., , ) the first time you link an empty folder, then shows the user-picker screen. After you pick or create a username, the in-product BeakerBot tour auto-fires on the empty account. That tour is a different thing from the walkth...

ResearchOS uses the File System Access API, which is only available in Chrome and Edge. Safari, Firefox, and Brave are not supported (Brave disables the API on purpose). See Browser Requirements for details.

Clicking Take the 3-minute walkthrough opens a 4-beat modal (welcome, data security, folder choice, cloud provider). It runs the BeakerBot intro in slide form, then closes and returns you to this same picker. The modal never picks a folder for you. Returning users skip it enti...

If Chrome says a folder "contains system files" after you pick it, that is its block on sensitive locations: the top-level Desktop, Documents, Downloads, and home folders are off limits. The picker shows a recovery popup ( That folder can't be used. Pick a different spot. ) wi...
If you've used ResearchOS before, point it at the same folder you used last time. Your projects, tasks, methods, and notes will load exactly as you left them.
Skip the folder picker and visit Demo Mode instead. It opens the app at with a seeded fake yeast lab you can click around in. No folder, no install, edits disappear on reload.
If you clear the site's data (e.g., browser settings → clear data), the folder handle is wiped. Your data on disk is untouched. You just have to pick the folder again next time you open ResearchOS.
The first screen you see on a fresh install: one centered Link a folder card, BeakerBot's optional walkthrough nudge in the upper-right, and the RISE credentials stamp pinned bottom-right.

The folder-link screen with a single Link a folder card in the center, BeakerBot waving from the upper-right with a Take the 3-minute walkthrough button, an Explore demo in browser link below the card, and the RISE credentials stamp in the bottom-right corner.

entries.title: Creating a User
headings: PI accounts always have a password
bodySnippets: Each person in the folder picks a unique username. Their data lives under users/<username>/.
ResearchOS has no central account system. Each "user" is a named folder inside in your data directory, with their own projects, tasks, methods, and notes underneath.

In this model, the folder is the lab . Anyone with access to the same folder can pick a username on the user-picker and become a member, no invite step needed. If a folder is private to you, you're a lab of one. If a folder is shared via OneDrive / Google Drive / Dropbox / iCl...

If you share a laptop, or you're storing the folder in a shared cloud, you can set a per-user password. Go to Settings → Security → Set Password . The password is PBKDF2-hashed and stored in . You can also set or change a password directly from the user-picker by hovering a us...
Click your avatar in the top-right of the header, then Switch user . Pick another user from the list. Your view and data reload to that user's namespace.

One user can be marked as the main user . They get a star badge on their avatar in the picker and are auto-selected as the default on the next visit. To set a main user, hover any user row on the picker and click the star (outline) icon that appears on the right. The main badg...

To rename a user, hover their row on the user-picker and click the pencil (edit) icon. The row switches to an inline input with the current username pre-filled. Edit the name and press Enter or click the green checkmark to save. Press Escape or the red X to cancel. The same us...

To delete a user, hover their row and click the trash icon. A two-step confirmation dialog appears with an optional "Archive data before deletion" checkbox (checked by default). The archive step downloads a ZIP of the user's folder before the directory is removed.

On the user-picker screen, click + Create New User and type a username in the input field. The app accepts letters, numbers, and underscores only (e.g., , ). Hyphens are not accepted and will trigger a validation error.
Click Create & Login .
A directory at is created and you're signed in. The app initializes a full subdirectory tree inside it: , , , , , , , , , , , , and .

Brand-new users land in BeakerBot's welcome tour, a short guided walkthrough on your real account. Plan on roughly five to ten minutes, depending on which features you opt into. See Welcome Tour (BeakerBot) for the full walkthrough, or skip it and explore on your own.

Forgot your password? Delete from your folder (in Finder or Explorer) and the password gate goes away. Your data is untouched. This means a password isn't real security, it's a deterrent on a shared machine.

The optional password above is for members and solo accounts. A PI (an account flagged ) is the exception, a PI must set a password during account setup before they can finish, and it's enforced at login. So there's never a PI account with no password behind it. See Edit sessi...

The user-picker screen, shown right after you connect a folder. (Screenshot pending recapture; the Lab Mode button shown in older captures has been removed and the BeakerBot brand mark added. Use ?wikiCapture=1 when recapturing.)
The user-picker screen with the + Create New User button, the existing-user list, and the BeakerBot brand mark in the gradient pill at the top.

entries.title: Demo Mode
headings: Reading the docs from inside the demo
Three ways to try the app
Every name in the demo is invented
bodySnippets: An in-browser preview of ResearchOS, seeded with a fake yeast lab. No folder picker, nothing to install, edits disappear on reload.

Open (e.g., research-os-xi.vercel.app/demo , the canonical public URL as of this writing; check the project site for the current link if it has moved) and you land straight inside ResearchOS as a researcher named alex , with a populated lab to click around in. Nothing touches ...

The Home page opens with four projects, all prefixed DEMO: so it's obvious nothing here is real research. Each project has experiments on the Gantt, methods in the library, a couple of PCR protocols, a funded purchase list, and a few results pages with lab notes. Other seeded ...

Bare stays at in the URL bar and renders the Home page. Click any nav link (Gantt, Methods, Calendar) and the URL switches to the regular app path ( , ). Demo state follows you through those navigations via a sticky session flag, so the watermarked data and the demo affordance...
Four seeded users are available from the user-picker. Knowing who is who matters because the lab-head and shared-records walkthroughs depend on signing in as the right researcher.

Everything you do in (dragging a Gantt bar, editing a lab note, adding a task, dropping in an image) stays in this browser tab. There's no disk write, no upload, no account. Refresh the tab and the demo resets to its seed state. Good for kicking the tires, not for real research.

Every demo experiment, goal, and event gets its start and end date rebased on the fly so the demo's "today" matches your real today. Visit the demo this week and upcoming experiments stay upcoming, the Gantt scrolls to the current week, and the calendar shows events around now...

Method history, PCR protocol creation dates, and lab note timestamps stay put, because they read as history rather than schedule. The rebase is also idempotent, so visiting twice in the same day doesn't accumulate drift.
Two floating affordances stay with you across every route inside the demo, regardless of whether the URL is a path or the equivalent live app path ( , , and so on).

In the bottom-right corner, a darker pill-shaped Read the docs button (with a small upward-right arrow glyph) shows up whenever the current view has a matching wiki page. Click it on and you land on the Methods wiki entry, ready to read about what you just clicked. The button ...

The mirror move works from the wiki side. Feature pages with a demo-able view embed an amber inline call-out (the component) that drops you into for that view. Each call-site picks its own label, so you'll see variants like Try the Gantt view , Try methods in the demo , Try th...

A floating amber Leave Demo button lives in the bottom-right and follows you across every route while the demo flag is set. It's undismissable, so you never lose the exit. Clicking it opens a dialog titled Leave the demo? with two choices:
alex (member). The default sign-in. Owns all four DEMO: projects and is the user you start as on .
morgan (member). Shares one project with alex and drops comments into alex's threads, so any view that filters by shared records shows real cross-user activity.

Dr. Mira Castellanos (account_type ). The PI of the demo lab. Mira is the user to pick when exploring the lab-head walkthrough, the lab inbox, the PI audit log, the announcements, and the flag-for-review notifications. She owns the orange (#f97316) user color in the comment th...

Dr. Sam Whitley (member, archived). Sam was archived by mira on 2026-03-15 and exists to demo the user-archiving feature. His historical comments and methods are still visible across the lab, but he no longer shows up in assignment pickers.

The amber Leave demo button at the top resets all demo state in this tab and drops you on the connect-folder screen, ready to pick a real folder (or start a new one). Demo edits are not saved to disk anywhere, so they go away with the reload. If you opened from a tab that was ...
The smaller Keep exploring the demo link at the bottom just closes the dialog if you clicked Leave Demo by mistake.
(this page). Fastest. Open the URL, click around, no setup. Edits are temporary and vanish on reload. Best for a first look or for sharing a link with a labmate.

The "Or download as a starter folder" link. On the connect-folder screen there's a small text link under the big Explore demo in browser button. It downloads with the same seeded yeast lab. Unzip it, point ResearchOS at the unzipped folder, and your edits persist on disk for a...

Connect a real folder. For actual research work. See Connecting Your Folder for the picker flow, and Shared Lab Accounts for putting that folder in OneDrive / Drive / Dropbox / iCloud so your whole lab shares it.

The demo lab is full of fake-on-purpose labels: , , , and so on. If you decide to export the demo as a starter folder, rename the projects before you start filing real data in there so nothing reads as a tutorial leftover later.
The Leave Demo dialog. Confirm to reset and return to the folder picker, or keep exploring.
The Leave the demo dialog with a primary Leave demo button and a smaller Keep exploring link below.

entries.title: Exporting from LabArchives
headings: One ZIP can be re-imported later
bodySnippets: ResearchOS imports LabArchives notebooks by reading the Offline Notebook ZIP that LabArchives exports. This page walks you through producing that ZIP.

LabArchives is your ELN of record while you're running experiments. ResearchOS doesn't talk to LabArchives in real time, and it doesn't write anything back to your LabArchives account. The hand-off in between is a single file, the Offline Notebook ZIP , that LabArchives genera...

Once you have that ZIP downloaded, the ResearchOS importer reads it directly. You don't unzip it, and you don't need ResearchOS to be signed in to LabArchives. The next page over, Import from LabArchives , covers what the importer does with the file.
You don't need to open the ZIP yourself, but it helps to know what's in there:

Some inline images live in LabArchives' cloud as URLs rather than bundled bytes. The Offline Notebook ZIP keeps the URL but not the image itself for those. ResearchOS handles that on import through a separate Fetch images step, which the import page covers.
With the ZIP downloaded, head to Import from LabArchives to walk through the ResearchOS-side wizard. You'll pick the format, drop the ZIP, preview what's inside, map folders to projects, and apply.
One HTML file per notebook page, holding the page title, every entry on that page, and the entry text.
A subdirectory of attachments for each page, where image uploads and other bundled files sit alongside the HTML.
A top-level manifest that describes the notebook tree, the export timestamp, and who triggered the export.
Sign in to LabArchives at mynotebook.labarchives.com (or your institution's LabArchives URL) in a normal browser tab.

Open the notebook you want to bring into ResearchOS from the notebook switcher in the top-left. Export is one notebook at a time, so if you want several notebooks in ResearchOS, plan to repeat this flow for each.

Open the ≡ Tools menu in the top toolbar and pick Utilities → Create Offline Notebook . (Older builds park this under More → Offline Notebook or Notebook Settings → Export . You're after the same item either way.)
Confirm the request when LabArchives prompts. The request goes into a queue, and LabArchives builds the ZIP server-side. You can close the tab while it works.
Wait for the email. LabArchives mails you a link as soon as the ZIP is ready. Small notebooks take a minute or two, large ones with many attachments can take much longer.

Click the link in the email and download the ZIP to your computer. The file is usually named something like . Save it somewhere you can find later (your Downloads folder is fine), and do not unzip it . ResearchOS reads the archive as a single file.

LabArchives occasionally renames menu items or moves them between toolbars. If a label below doesn't match what you see on screen, look for the same underlying concept: you're asking LabArchives to produce an Offline Notebook export of a single notebook. Other names you might ...

ResearchOS's importer is idempotent against the same ZIP. If you re-run the import on the same file, pages you've already imported are skipped. If you export a fresher ZIP from LabArchives later and run it through, only the new pages land. See Re-running the import for the det...

entries.title: User archiving
