entries.title: Calendar Feeds
headings: Add a calendar by ICS URL
Where to copy the ICS URL from
What URLs the server proxy will fetch
Want to edit linked events from ResearchOS?
Secret URLs leak your whole calendar
bodySnippets: Overlay events from Google, Outlook, iCloud, or any public iCal URL on the ResearchOS calendar. Read-only, so you can plan around them without ResearchOS ever writing back to the source.

Each linked calendar shows its events on the Calendar page in the color you pick, sitting alongside ResearchOS tasks and events. Clicking an event opens a popup with the title, time, and any location or notes the source calendar published.

Linked calendars are read-only. ResearchOS fetches a public iCal (ICS) URL on a timer and overlays the events. It never writes back to Google, Outlook, or Apple. To edit an event, open it in its source app, and the change syncs into ResearchOS on the next 15-minute refresh.
Go to the Calendar page. The Linked Calendars button sits top-right, next to + New Event . A small blue badge on the button counts how many subscriptions are currently turned on.
Inside the panel, the Where do I find this URL? link expands a per-provider checklist that matches whichever Provider you have selected. The summaries below are the same steps in long form.
Pick Other (any public iCal URL) in the Provider dropdown. That covers university course schedules, lab seminar series, conference timetables, sports schedules, anything that ends in or starts with .
Each subscription becomes a row in the Connected list at the top of the panel. The row shows:

ResearchOS refetches each feed at most once every 15 minutes per browser session. The fetch goes through a small server proxy (the same Vercel function that bypasses CORS for Google, Outlook, and iCloud), and the proxy caches the response on its own edge cache for another 15 m...
If you just added an event in the source calendar and want it on the ResearchOS grid right now, hard-refresh the browser tab or wait out the 15-minute window.

The proxy is the small server endpoint that fetches each ICS URL on your behalf, because browsers can't fetch directly (Google doesn't set the right CORS headers). The proxy refuses a few classes of URL outright so it can't be used to scan internal infrastructure:
If a paste fails with one of those errors, double-check that the URL is the public ICS variant of the calendar (not the regular web-app share link).

Repeating events (e.g., a weekly seminar) are expanded into one chip per occurrence inside a ±2-year window around today. That keeps a 20-year-long daily recurrence from spawning ~7,300 chips on the grid. If you scroll the calendar more than two years backward or forward, repe...

Linked calendars are read-only by design. To make changes, open the event in its source app (Google Calendar, Outlook, Apple Calendar). Your edit shows up in ResearchOS on the next 15-minute refresh. Earlier ResearchOS builds had an OAuth-backed two-way sync for Google and Out...
Open in a desktop browser.
Click the gear -> Settings -> Settings for my calendars .
Pick the calendar you want to share in the sidebar.
Copy Secret address in iCal format for a private calendar, or Public address in iCal format if the calendar is already public.
Open Outlook on the web -> Settings -> Calendar -> Shared calendars .
Under Publish a calendar , pick the calendar, choose Can view all details , and click Publish .
Copy the ICS link Outlook generates.
Open the Calendar app on macOS, or sign in to in a browser.
Right-click the calendar in the sidebar -> Share Calendar -> Public Calendar .
Copy the share link. It starts with , which is fine to paste as-is.
The label you gave it, plus a small uppercase tag with the provider type.
The full ICS URL, truncated to one line.
A Last synced timestamp once ResearchOS has fetched the feed at least once.
A 5-swatch color strip on the left edge of the row. Click any swatch to recolor that calendar's events on the grid.
An On / Off checkbox to hide a calendar's events without removing the subscription.
A red Remove link to drop the subscription. A confirmation dialog reminds you that ResearchOS events aren't touched.
Anything that isn't (so no , no , no ). Plain is rejected outright for security. The prefix is rewritten to first, so iCloud URLs still work.
and the loopback range .
Private IPv4 ranges: , , .
Link-local and the IPv6 equivalents ( , , ).
Responses that don't start with . This catches the case where a calendar share URL redirects to an HTML sign-in page instead of returning iCal text.
Click Linked Calendars , then scroll the panel down to Or paste a public iCal / ICS URL .
Pick the source from the Provider dropdown (iCloud / Apple Calendar, Google Calendar, Outlook / Office 365, or Other (any public iCal URL)).
Type a Label (this is what shows up on the Calendar page) and click one of the 10 swatches under Color .
Paste the URL into the ICS URL field and click Add Calendar . The button shows Testing... while ResearchOS fetches the feed to confirm it parses.
If the feed parses but contains no events within a ±2-year window of today, you'll get a confirmation dialog before the subscription saves. That's the normal case for stale or empty calendars.
Paste a URL exactly as iCloud (or any other client) gives it to you. The server proxy rewrites it to automatically before fetching.

Google's Secret address in iCal format grants read access to every event title, time, and note in that calendar to anyone who has the URL. Treat it like a password (e.g., don't paste it into Slack, a public repo, or a shared notes doc). ResearchOS keeps the URL in your private...

The edge cache also serves the previous response while fetching a fresh copy in the background (stale-while-revalidate, up to 1 hour). In the worst case an event you just added could take up to 75 minutes to appear (15 min client window + 60 min stale window). Hard-refreshing ...
The Linked Calendars panel with a connected ICS subscription and the paste-a-URL form.

entries.title: LabArchives
headings: Exporting an Offline Notebook ZIP from LabArchives
Path 2: Manual folder or ZIP drop
Skip image fetch on the first pass
bodySnippets: Bring notebooks out of LabArchives and into ResearchOS as native, file-on-disk tasks — and recover the inline images that LabArchives keeps in the cloud, all without any API credentials.

LabArchives and ResearchOS sit at opposite ends of a hand-off, not in a live sync. LabArchives is your ELN of record while you're running experiments; ResearchOS is where you bring those notebooks once you want them on disk in a structured form. There's no two-way connection, ...
Everything runs through one entry point under Settings -> LabArchives :

LabArchives stores inline images in two ways internally. The legacy path, called Form-A , embeds the image as a binary file inside the notebook page entry itself. The newer path, Form-B , uploads the image to LabArchives' cloud and leaves only a URL behind in the entry body.

When you generate an Offline Notebook export ZIP, only the Form-A images come along. The Form-B URLs are still in the entry text, but the image bytes were never bundled. In a typical recent notebook that's roughly half the inline images.

If you import the ZIP without fetching Form-B images, the wizard writes a placeholder for each one. Your notes still come across; the images at those URLs simply aren't there yet. You can recover them at any time using one of the three paths described below, or remove individu...
When images are recovered, inline images land in the task's folder and the markdown is rewritten to point at the local copy. File attachments (non-image uploads) land in .
The ZIP that ResearchOS imports is LabArchives' built-in Offline Notebook export. Both the import and any image-recovery run depend on having this file first.
Three paths exist to pull Form-B images into your notes. All three are credential-free: ResearchOS never asks for your LabArchives password.

The Generate browser script tab (in the wizard's Fetch images step, or in the per-task Rehydrate panel) shows a self-contained JavaScript snippet you paste into the browser console while you're logged into LabArchives. The script:
Drop the downloaded ZIP into the Drop your own images tab (or drag it straight into the panel) and ResearchOS matches filenames automatically.

If you already have the Form-B image files on disk (from a previous download, a screenshot batch, or an export from another tool), use the Drop your own images tab. Drop a folder or a containing the image files and ResearchOS matches them to the expected filenames automaticall...

This path also accepts the ZIP produced by the DevTools script above, which is why the two-tab flow is designed the way it is: generate and download in the DevTools tab, then drop the result in the manual tab.
After import, any task that still has unresolved Form-B images shows broken image placeholders in the Lab Notes editor. Clicking a broken placeholder opens a small popup with three options:

Import from LabArchives — the import wizard. You download an Offline Notebook ZIP from LabArchives and feed it to the wizard. Every notebook page becomes a ResearchOS task; every folder becomes a project you can map to your existing project list. No credentials, works offline.
Generate browser script — the DevTools-script path (see below).
Drop your own images — the manual folder/ZIP drop path (see below).
Checks that it's running on — it refuses to do anything on other origins.
Fetches each missing image URL using your existing session cookies (no password, no API key — just the session the browser already has from your login).
Packages all successful fetches into a single ZIP and triggers one browser download.

Find on LabArchives — opens the original image URL in a new tab pointed at . If you're logged in, the image loads and you can right-click -> Save. Then use Replace from disk below, or drag the saved file onto the note.

Replace from disk — opens a file picker. Pick a local image file; ResearchOS writes it to , rewrites the markdown reference, and removes the entry from the task's sidecar so the popup doesn't re-appear on the next open.
Remove reference — deletes the broken placeholder from the markdown entirely. Use this when you don't have the image and don't need it.
Open the notebook you want to export in LabArchives on the web.
Click More in the top toolbar -> Offline Notebook . (LabArchives sometimes labels this Export Notebook or Backup Notebook depending on your institution's build.)
Wait. Large notebooks take minutes to assemble; LabArchives emails you when the ZIP is ready.
Download the ZIP from the email link. Don't unzip it — ResearchOS reads the raw archive.
Go to Settings -> LabArchives and click Open import... on the Import from LabArchives card.
Drag the Offline Notebook ZIP into the upload step. The wizard parses the bundle and surfaces a preview of pages, folders, and inline images.
On the Pick format step, leave the default (Offline Notebook). PDF and Chrome-print formats are stubbed for a later version.
On the Preview step, glance through the page list and confirm the entry counts look right.
On the Project mapping step, point each LabArchives folder at one of your existing ResearchOS projects, or let the wizard create a new project named .

If the wizard detected Form-B images (and you're not in demo mode), a Fetch images step appears. It shows two tabs: Generate browser script — the DevTools-script path (see below). Drop your own images — the manual folder/ZIP drop path (see below). Either fetch images now or cl...
The Apply step writes everything to disk. When it's finished, the Done step lists what landed where.
In the Fetch images wizard step (or per-task Rehydrate panel), click the Generate browser script tab and copy the script.
Open LabArchives in your browser and navigate to any page of the notebook you imported. You just need to be logged in.
Open DevTools (F12 or right-click -> Inspect), switch to the Console tab, paste the script, and press Enter.
The script logs progress in the console and triggers a single ZIP download when done. Save the ZIP somewhere easy to find.
Switch back to ResearchOS and drop the ZIP into the Drop your own images tab. The panel shows how many filenames matched and stages them for import.
Click Continue with N images . The Apply step writes them to and rewrites the markdown references.

It's fine to skip image recovery during the initial import. Form-B images become placeholders you can clean up later. The broken-image popup in any imported note lets you handle them one at a time, and the per-task "Rehydrate images" panel lets you batch them all at once.

Each notebook page becomes a directory under with a body, a folder for file attachments, a folder for inline images, and an for provenance. Tasks are marked complete, with and set to the page's newest entry timestamp.

The script runs entirely in your browser. ResearchOS never sees the image bytes until you explicitly drop the resulting ZIP back into the wizard. The URLs are hard-coded into the script (no dynamic eval, no network round-trip back to ResearchOS). The flag only attaches cookies...

If you saved an image via "Find on LabArchives" and it filename-matches an outstanding placeholder, dragging it straight onto the note triggers the same recovery pipeline as the file picker — no need to open the popup first.

entries.title: Telegram Bot
headings: When both an experiment and a note are open
Pairing isn't saved until you send your bot a message
During setup, your first photo just goes to the Inbox
Photos render inline, even with spaces in the name
Right-click works on a single row too
bodySnippets: Pair your account with a Telegram bot so phone photos from the bench are routed to the right experiment with a single tap.

Once paired, you send photos to a single Telegram bot from your phone. Where a photo lands depends on one thing: what you have open in ResearchOS when it arrives. The routing is deliberately small, two cases instead of a long destination menu:

When you pick Lab Notes or Results, the bot then asks for a caption. Type one back and it is stored alongside the image. Send to leave the photo without one. Photos that go straight to the Inbox skip the caption round, since sorting (and captioning) happens in the app.

At the top of every page in ResearchOS there's a small pill that either reads "Connect Telegram" (gray, when no bot is paired) or with a green pulsing dot when polling is healthy. The pill turns amber if another tab is already polling the same bot, and red if Telegram has reje...

If the bridge hits a transient hiccup like a token refresh blip, a flaky Wi-Fi connection, or a brief Telegram API timeout, the pill switches to amber with a pulsing dot and adds a small label next to the bot username. The poller backs off and retries on its own, doubling the ...

Each time a new photo arrives over Telegram while this tab is open, the pill grows a green counter on the right (e.g., after three photos have come in). It's a quick confirmation that the bridge is alive and that the photos you just sent from your phone actually made it across...

Routing keys off what you have open in ResearchOS when the photo arrives. There's no big destination menu anymore. The old "pick from a list of every experiment" picker was removed: sorting now happens in a beefed-up in-app Inbox (see below), so the bot only ever has to handle...
When an experiment is open, the bot's only question is Lab Notes or Results, shown as two buttons. One tap commits the destination.

Notes are first-class destinations alongside experiments. When a note popup is the only thing open in ResearchOS, the photo attaches straight to that note. Notes do not have a Lab Notes vs Results split, so there's nothing to pick. The photo lands in and a markdown image link ...

If you have an experiment popup AND a note popup open at the same time, the experiment wins. The bot runs the usual Lab Notes / Results question for that experiment. If you actually meant the note, close the experiment popup first, or let the photo go to the Inbox and file it ...

Whenever a photo arrives with nothing open in ResearchOS, it lands at and the Inbox badge in the top bar increments. This is now the main sorting surface: instead of the bot making you choose a destination from a long list on your phone, you batch your unsorted photos in the I...
When you come back to your desk with a stack of phone photos that all belong to the same experiment, the inbox lets you file them in one move instead of clicking Move to active on each row:

With one or more rows selected, right-click any selected row (or click the ⋯ button) to open a context menu. The top two items read Send to task... and Send to note... for a single row, or Send N items to task... / Send N items to note... when multiple are selected. The task p...

The reply depends on what is open. With an experiment open, the bot asks Lab Notes or Results ; after you tap, it saves the file and asks for a caption. Reply with a sentence and it is stored as the image's caption, or send to leave the photo without one. With nothing open, th...

Albums (multiple photos sent together) are handled as one batch rather than prompted per photo. With an experiment open, you answer Lab Notes or Results once for the whole batch, then name the batch in one go or caption each photo individually. With nothing open, the whole bat...
Send to the bot at any time for a refresher on how it behaves.
Click the Telegram pill again. The modal lists the current pairing with a Disconnect bot button. Clicking it deletes your local and polling stops. Existing photos and notes aren't touched.
The bot itself stays alive on Telegram's side. To delete the bot entirely, send to @BotFather and confirm with the bot's username.

Polling Telegram from two ResearchOS tabs at once would have them fight over the same message cursor. The app holds a cross-tab lock in , so only one tab polls at a time. The other tabs show an amber pill with the label and pick up automatically if the active tab closes.
An experiment is open. The bot asks one quick question, Lab Notes or Results , and saves the photo to that experiment once you tap.
Nothing is open. The photo goes straight to your Inbox and the bot just replies "Saved to inbox." No buttons, no picker. You sort it later from the in-app Inbox.
An experiment is open in ResearchOS. The bot asks one question: Lab Notes (saves into 's image folder) or Results (saves into the image folder). Tap one and it lands in that experiment.
Only a note popup is open. The photo attaches straight to that note, no Lab Notes / Results split (notes don't have one).
Nothing is open. The photo goes straight to your Inbox and the bot replies "Saved to inbox." No buttons. Open the in-app Inbox to file it whenever you are back at your desk.
Lab Notes routes the photo into the folder under .
Results routes it into the folder under .
Shift-click a second row to select the contiguous range from your last anchor row to this one.
Cmd-click (or Ctrl-click on Windows / Linux) to toggle individual rows in and out of the selection without disturbing the rest.
Selected rows pick up a blue border and ring. Clicking the empty area of the modal clears the selection.
Open Telegram on your phone or desktop and start a chat with @BotFather .
Send . BotFather first asks for a display name (anything you want), then a username (must end in , e.g., ).
BotFather replies with the bot's API token, a string like . Copy it.
Click the Connect Telegram pill in the top bar of any page in ResearchOS.
Paste the bot token into the Bot token field and click Connect . The modal validates the token with Telegram and shows the bot's username.
On your phone, open a chat with your new bot and send any message (e.g., ). ResearchOS picks up the message, captures your chat id, and the modal flips to a green "Paired" confirmation.

Send any photo to the bot. With nothing open in ResearchOS it lands in your Inbox within a couple of seconds and the bot replies "Saved to inbox." Open an experiment first and the bot will instead ask Lab Notes or Results.

The "send a message" step is how the bot learns which Telegram chat belongs to you. Until that first message arrives, the modal sits at the "waiting" screen. You can cancel from that screen at any time.

While you are still walking through setup, the bot keeps it extra simple: the first photo you send lands in your Inbox with a short acknowledgement, no questions, so you can confirm the bridge works before you have your bearings. Once you finish the walkthrough, the bot switch...

Whatever the photo ends up named, once it lands in Lab Notes or Results it shows up inline in the markdown rather than as a broken link. Filenames with spaces (a phone document called , say, or a batch name you typed) used to trip up the markdown image reference and quietly dr...

You don't need to select multiple rows first. Right-click any inbox row and pick Send to task... to file that one photo into any experiment, even one you don't have open. It saves opening the experiment's popup just to use the Move to active button.

Anyone with this token can send messages and receive photos as your bot. ResearchOS writes the token to in your data folder and auto-appends a rule so it isn't committed if the data folder is a git repo. Treat the token like a password.
Click the Telegram pill in the top bar to open this pairing modal.
The Telegram pairing modal with the bot-token field, opened from the Telegram pill in the top bar.
The Inbox modal, opened from the Inbox badge in the top bar.
The Inbox modal listing photos sent via Telegram with their captions, timestamps, and Move to active / Delete buttons.

entries.title: Security
headings: What briefly touches a server we operate
What we collect, and what we don't
Two recovery surfaces for your Telegram bot token
Public hosting is opt-in, and the proxies are open
The worst case is your last good save
bodySnippets: Your research data stays on your computer. We never see it. This page is the plain-English version of what that means in practice, where the narrow exceptions are, and how you can verify the claim for yourself.

ResearchOS is built as a browser-only app that reads and writes a folder you pick on your own machine. Your notes, results, project and task data, images, attachments, Telegram inbox, and calendar subscriptions all stay in that folder. Nothing about your work is uploaded to a ...

When you pick a data folder, ResearchOS reads and writes everything directly to that folder on your machine. The bytes never leave it. You can open the folder at any time in your operating system's file browser and see exactly what is there.
The folder holds:

Three routes on the ResearchOS server are involved. The first two are CORS-bypass streams that exist because browsers refuse to talk directly to the upstream services. The third is an anonymous page-view ping. None of them ever sees the contents of your data folder.

The two CORS-bypass proxy routes use the most defensive shape we know how to write: HTTPS only, private-IP blocking, redirect re-validation, byte cap, timeout, content-type denylist, and per-IP rate limiting. The Vercel Analytics and Speed Insights endpoints are Vercel-owned s...

The on-disk sidecar can disappear for ordinary reasons: a misshared OneDrive deletion, an iCloud sync hiccup, a lab-mate tidying up. ResearchOS keeps two optional recovery paths so you don't have to start over from BotFather when that happens. Both live on your own machine. Ne...

The first recovery path is a browser-scoped IndexedDB store ( ) that holds a minimal credential row so the app can offer a one-click recovery prompt instead of making you re-pair. The cache stores only , , and , keyed by .

The cache is symmetric in risk with the disk sidecar: both are DevTools-readable on the local machine, and the cache is not exposed via cloud-folder share (browser-scoped, not file-scoped). That's a small win over disk-only storage for the multi-user-folder case: Alice's cache...

The only network call adjacent to the cache is a round-trip to to confirm the cached token still works before the recovery prompt offers it. That call goes direct from your browser to Telegram, the same path the bot polling already uses. The Forget button in Settings -> Data i...

The second recovery path is an encrypted backup file on disk, off by default. If you turn it on, either via the checkbox in the Telegram pairing dialog or via Settings -> Notifications & behavior -> Auto-reconnect Telegram bot , ResearchOS writes an encrypted file at next to y...

Lose your account password and you lose the backup. There is no recovery key, no escape hatch, and no copy of your password anywhere in ResearchOS (we never had it). Anyone who knows your account password can also decrypt this backup, so treat the encrypted backup and your acc...

Changing your account password through the normal change- password flow handles the re-encryption automatically. The submit button label flips to "Re-encrypting Telegram backup..." while it runs, and the success banner reads "Password updated. Telegram backup re-encrypted." Th...
The encryption parameters (KDF, cipher, IV / salt sizes, fail-closed decryption semantics) live in SECURITY_AUDIT.md sections 1.5 and 1.6 for readers who want the bytes.

We collect anonymous page-view pings via Vercel Web Analytics and anonymous Core Web Vitals (load and responsiveness timings) via Vercel Speed Insights. When you navigate between pages, anonymous beacons go to Vercel. No IDs, no folder contents, no typed text, no markdown bodi...

We do not collect anything else. No Sentry, no Google Analytics, no Mixpanel, no PostHog, no Hotjar, no Datadog, no Amplitude. No background phone-home. No crash reporter. No content telemetry. Running against the repo will confirm only and are present, and the network tab wil...

The Report an issue button does not auto-submit anything. When you click it, your browser opens a pre-filled GitHub issue URL in a new tab. You see the body, edit it, and click Submit . Nothing happens until you do.
These claims hold, but they have implications worth understanding before you trust ResearchOS with anything sensitive.
You don't have to take any of this on faith. There are two ways to confirm what the app is doing. The first is built into Settings and takes about thirty seconds.
Your browser already shows every network request the app makes. This is the audit-grade path for anyone who wants to see the bytes themselves.

ResearchOS went through an internal security audit on 2026-05-15. It closed one Critical finding (cross-site scripting via unsanitized markdown HTML across all 8 markdown rendering sites in the app) and several Important findings around the proxy routes, the LabArchives creden...
If you find something that looks wrong, open a GitHub issue (the Report an issue button is the fastest path) or email the maintainer directly. We'd much rather hear about a problem early.

A subtler kind of data loss has nothing to do with the network and everything to do with timing. If an app is halfway through writing a file when the tab closes, the laptop sleeps, or the process crashes, a naive implementation can leave the file zero-byte or truncated, and th...

Every save writes to a temporary file first, lets that file finish landing durably on disk, and only then atomically moves it into place over the real file. The move is the kind of operation that either fully happens or does not happen at all, with no in-between state on disk....

A trust claim is only as good as the thing that keeps it true over time. The whole app is gated by automated tests that run on every commit and every pull request to : linting, a full TypeScript typecheck, the Vitest unit and integration suite with coverage, and Playwright end...
Every experiment, lab note, and result you write.
Every image, PDF, and arbitrary attachment you drop into a note.
The JSON for projects, tasks, dependencies, methods, and PCR protocols.
Your Telegram bot token and any photos that arrived through it.
Your calendar subscription URLs and the events they pulled in.
The optional PBKDF2-hashed password protecting your per-user profile.

Calendar feed sync. When you subscribe to an iCal URL (Google, Outlook, iCloud, a university calendar), the browser asks to fetch the iCal text from the upstream and stream it back. The subscription URL travels in the request header rather than in the URL query string, so it i...

Telegram file CDN. When a photo arrives through your bot, the browser asks to fetch the bytes from Telegram (whose CDN refuses to set CORS headers, so the browser cannot reach it directly). The bot token travels in a request header, never in the URL. The bytes stream straight ...

Vercel Web Analytics. When you navigate between pages, your browser sends an anonymous page-view beacon to Vercel telling them which route you visited. No IDs, no folder contents, no typed text, no markdown bodies, no project names. Vercel sees your IP address (which they hash...
The Data inventory panel lists every file path the app has written and every IndexedDB key in use, alongside an External Calls section that names each outbound endpoint in plain English.
The Offline mode toggle disables the two proxy routes plus the Vercel Analytics and Speed Insights script tags in one click, for anyone who wants zero outbound network from the app surface.
"Where is this stored?" hints on each integration field so you always know which file holds the credential you just entered.
The first, , holds the opaque File System Access handle for your data folder. This is what gives the browser permission to read and write the folder you picked.
The second, , holds three small session-routing strings: the folder name plus its grant timestamp, the currently signed-in user, and (if you are a PI signed in) the primary account.
The third, , exists only if you have paired a Telegram bot. It holds one row per paired-user-in-this-folder, with the minimal credentials , , , keyed by .
Open ResearchOS in Chrome or Edge, then open DevTools. F12 works on Windows and Linux. Cmd + Option + I works on macOS.
Switch to the Network tab and check Preserve log so refreshes don't clear the view.
Reload the page. Visit Calendar, Telegram inbox, the experiments you care about. Watch every outbound request as you go.

You should see requests to your own ResearchOS origin (for JavaScript, CSS, and static assets), occasional requests to when a subscribed feed refreshes, occasional requests to when an inbox photo loads, direct requests to when you exchange messages with your bot, and occasiona...

For a second pass, switch from the Network tab to Application -> IndexedDB . ResearchOS uses three IndexedDB databases. The first, , holds the opaque File System Access handle for your data folder. This is what gives the browser permission to read and write the folder you pick...

Open your data folder in Finder or Explorer. Every experiment, note, image, and attachment you have written is sitting there as a regular file you can read. ResearchOS writes to that folder and nowhere else. Quit the app and your data stays exactly where it is.

The first recovery path is a browser-scoped IndexedDB store ( ) that holds a minimal credential row so the app can offer a one-click recovery prompt instead of making you re-pair. The cache stores only , , and , keyed by . The cache is symmetric in risk with the disk sidecar: ...

The second recovery path is an encrypted backup file on disk, off by default. If you turn it on, either via the checkbox in the Telegram pairing dialog or via Settings -> Notifications & behavior -> Auto-reconnect Telegram bot , ResearchOS writes an encrypted file at next to y...

If you share your data folder with a lab-mate over OneDrive, Dropbox, Google Drive, or iCloud, every byte in it is theirs to read. The per-user password gate stops accidental account-switching inside the app, not someone opening the folder in their own file browser.

We hash account passwords with PBKDF2-SHA-256 (600,000 iterations) so a snooping co-located user can't trivially read them, but the data itself sits in plaintext on disk. Anyone with disk access has it. Turn on OS-level full-disk encryption (FileVault on macOS, BitLocker on Wi...

Your Telegram bot token lives in plaintext in . We auto-gitignore the file so it doesn't slip into a , but anyone who reads the file can post and read messages on your bot's chats. Treat it like any other API key. The optional encrypted backup at (described above) is the one p...

If you self-host ResearchOS on a public Vercel deploy with no auth gate, anyone on the internet can hit the two proxy routes (subject to rate limiting). This is not a data-exfiltration risk because the proxies have no access to your data folder, but it does mean someone could ...

ResearchOS ships three affordances inside Settings: The Data inventory panel lists every file path the app has written and every IndexedDB key in use, alongside an External Calls section that names each outbound endpoint in plain English. The Offline mode toggle disables the t...

A torn write can only ever leave the old file contents intact, never a corrupt or empty one. The atomic move is the whole point. Either the new version replaces the old one cleanly, or the old one is still there.
The Report an Issue modal. You see the body, you edit the body, you choose whether to submit.

The Report an Issue modal, showing the Type radio group, an editable Title field, an editable description, optional auto-attached error details, and the Cancel / Copy Link / Create GitHub Issue buttons at the bottom.

entries.title: Shared Lab Accounts
headings: Setup steps (every member runs these once)
Want to feel out the shared model first?
The one rule: keep the folder available offline
The PI sees the lab roll-up
bodySnippets: One folder, shared across your whole lab. Everyone keeps their own data, and the PI's dashboard rolls it all up.

Put your ResearchOS folder inside a cloud-synced folder (e.g., OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, or iCloud). Every lab member points ResearchOS at that same folder on their own computer. Inside the folder, each member picks a different username, so their data lives at , , and s...
The cloud provider handles syncing files between machines. ResearchOS has no idea any of this is happening, it just reads and writes JSON files like normal.
Before you start using ResearchOS, open the shared folder in Finder (macOS) or Explorer (Windows) and confirm:
Files show as fully downloaded (i.e., no cloud icon, no "online only" badge, no "available when online" tag).
You can open a sample file with the network disconnected.
The folder's "Size on disk" in its properties dialog is non-zero and roughly matches the "Size".
Don't put the folder in two different cloud providers. Pick one. Stacking OneDrive and Dropbox on the same folder corrupts JSON files when both try to write.
Don't use the same username on two laptops. If Sarah signs in as on her laptop and also on a shared lab computer at the same time, the two ResearchOS sessions will overwrite each other's files.
Don't edit files outside ResearchOS while the app is open. The app caches data in memory, so external edits won't be picked up until you reload the page.
Install the cloud provider's desktop app (not just the website). OneDrive on Windows is pre-installed. Drive for desktop, Dropbox, and iCloud Drive each ship as a separate download.
One person creates an empty folder named e.g. inside the synced area, then shares it with the rest of the lab via the cloud provider's normal share flow.
Each member accepts the share and confirms the folder is now visible on their laptop's filesystem (Finder / Explorer).
Each member follows their provider's instructions below to make the folder always available offline .
Each member opens ResearchOS, clicks Link Folder , and picks that shared folder.
On the user-picker, each member clicks Create New User and types their own username.

Open Demo Mode for a no-setup preview. It seeds two researchers (alex and morgan) plus their PI (Mira) into one shared folder, so cross-user data and the PI rollup are already wired up for you to click around in.

ResearchOS NEEDS to read and write the folder directly on disk. If your cloud provider keeps the folder "online only" (i.e., a placeholder file that downloads on demand), writes will fail or be silently dropped. EVERY member needs to flip the "always keep local" switch for the...

Once everyone is set up, the PI sees a customizable dashboard at with widgets for cross-lab activity, purchases, member workload, announcements, and more. See Lab Overview . If you only want one labmate's work surfaced on your own Home page (rather than the whole lab), they ca...

entries.title: Box
headings: Make it Available Offline (every member, every laptop)
Where the folder lives on disk
Enterprise policy can block Make Available Offline
bodySnippets: Box Drive streams files on demand. Mark the ResearchOS folder Available Offline so it stays on disk.

Download Box Drive from box.com/resources/downloads . You need the desktop client running. The web interface at is not enough. ResearchOS needs the desktop app because that's what creates the local filesystem folder you point ResearchOS at.

Box Drive streams files on demand by default, so the folder shows up as a placeholder until you open something. ResearchOS reads directly from disk, so anything still cloud-only breaks. You need to mark the lab folder as available offline on every laptop.
Windows : under (e.g. ).
macOS : under on recent macOS versions, or on older installs.

Files revert to cloud-only. If a colleague opens the shared folder's properties and chooses Free up space , the folder switches back to streaming and ResearchOS can't read it. Re-run Make Available Offline .

Conflict files. If two members edit the same file while offline, Box creates a copy named next to the original. Delete the conflicted copy in Finder / Explorer. ResearchOS only reads the canonical name.

Collaborator vs. owner storage. Shared folders on Box count against the folder owner's quota, not each collaborator's. Pick someone with enough quota (usually the lab's primary Box account) to own the folder.

Single sign-on re-auth. University Box accounts on SSO occasionally drop the Box Drive session and stop syncing without an obvious error. If files stop updating, open Box Drive's menu-bar / tray icon and confirm you're still signed in.
Installing Box Drive (Box Support)
Making files and folders available offline with Box Drive (Box Support)
Inviting collaborators to a folder (Box Support)
One member creates an empty folder inside their Box account (e.g., ). The folder can live anywhere under .
Right-click the folder in Box Drive (or open it on box.com) → Share . Invite lab members by email with Editor access. Co-owner works too if you want them to be able to re-share.
Each invited member accepts the invite. The folder appears under their Box Drive mount on disk (see "Where the folder lives on disk" below).
Open File Explorer (Windows) or Finder (macOS) and navigate to the shared lab folder under your Box mount.

Right-click the folder. In the context menu, choose Make Available Offline (Windows) or Available Offline (macOS). A green check icon replaces the cloud icon on the folder and on every file inside it.
Wait for Box Drive's menu-bar / tray icon to report "All files up to date" before opening ResearchOS. Large labs can take a while on the first sync.
Open ResearchOS, click Link Folder , and pick that shared folder.

Box Sync is the older client and has been retired. Make sure you're installing Box Drive . Box Drive shows every folder you have access to as a virtual filesystem and downloads files on demand, whereas Box Sync mirrored selected folders to disk. The instructions below assume B...
The folder picker can take up to a minute to open for Box folders. This is normal; Box Drive needs time to enumerate the folder tree. Wait for it rather than clicking again.

Some Box Enterprise admins disable Make Available Offline for compliance reasons. If you right-click the folder and don't see the option, or it's greyed out, ask your IT admin to enable offline access for your account. Without it, ResearchOS will hit empty placeholder files in...

entries.title: Dropbox
headings: Set Smart Sync to Local (every member, every laptop)
Smart Sync isn't on every plan
Or keep all of Dropbox local
bodySnippets: Dropbox's Smart Sync can store files online-only. Set the ResearchOS folder to Local so it stays on disk.
Download the desktop app from dropbox.com/install . You need the desktop client running. The web interface alone won't expose Dropbox as a folder ResearchOS can read.
Default to Online-only. Some Business plans default new shared folders to Online-only . Check the team admin settings if Smart Sync keeps reverting.
Selective Sync vs. Smart Sync. Selective Sync hides the folder entirely from your disk, while Smart Sync keeps a placeholder. You want Smart Sync set to Local , not Selective Sync excluded.

LAN sync conflicts. If two members on the same network edit the same file in quick succession, Dropbox can produce "conflicted copy" files. These appear next to the original. Delete the conflicted copy in Finder / Explorer. ResearchOS only reads the canonical name.
How to use Smart Sync (Dropbox Help)
Share files and folders (Dropbox Help)
One member creates an empty folder inside their Dropbox (e.g., ).
Right-click the folder → Share . Add lab members by email with Can edit permission.
Each invited member accepts the share. The folder appears under their local Dropbox tree on disk.
Open File Explorer (Windows) or Finder (macOS) and navigate to the shared lab folder inside Dropbox.
Right-click the folder → Smart Sync → Local . Every file gets downloaded and the cloud icon disappears.
Wait for Dropbox's menu-bar / tray icon to report "Up to date" before opening ResearchOS.
Open ResearchOS, click Link Folder , and pick that shared folder.

Smart Sync is included on Dropbox Plus, Family, Professional, and Business plans. On the free Basic plan, all files are local by default, so you can skip the Smart Sync step entirely. Just watch your storage quota.

If you're on a paid plan and want every Dropbox folder to stay on disk by default, open the Dropbox menu-bar / tray icon → Preferences -> Sync -> New folder default and set it to Local . Existing folders keep their current setting; only newly-added folders inherit the new defa...

entries.title: Google Drive
headings: Mark the lab folder Available offline (every member, every laptop)
Where the folder lives on disk
Or switch the whole Drive to Mirror
bodySnippets: Install Drive for desktop and mark the lab folder Available offline. That keeps only the lab folder on disk without pulling your whole Drive local.

Download Drive for desktop for Windows or macOS. The browser interface at is not enough. ResearchOS needs the desktop app because that's what creates the local filesystem folder you point ResearchOS at.

ResearchOS reads directly from disk, so anything still in Stream mode breaks. Most labs don't want the whole Drive switched to Mirror (your unrelated personal files, downloads, photos all get pulled local). Instead, mark just the lab folder as available offline.
Drive doesn't carry the "available offline" flag across machines, so every member has to do this on every laptop they use ResearchOS from.
Windows : usually mounted as drive or under .
macOS : under or shown as a volume on the desktop.

Stream mode by accident. If files in the lab folder show a cloud icon or take seconds to open, the folder isn't marked offline yet. Right-click the lab folder and pick Available offline (or switch your whole Drive to Mirror if you prefer that).
"Shared with me" is not synced. A shared folder doesn't appear in your local Drive folder until you Add shortcut to Drive . Without that, ResearchOS has nothing to point at.
Workspace admins sometimes lock Mirror mode off. Check with your IT admin if Drive for desktop only shows the Stream option.
Use files offline with Drive for desktop (Google Help)
Share folders in Google Drive (Google Help)
One member creates an empty folder in their Google Drive (e.g., ).
Right-click the folder → Share . Add lab members by email with Editor access.

Each invited member opens drive.google.com, finds the folder under Shared with me , right-clicks it, and selects Add shortcut to Drive → My Drive . This is what makes the folder show up on disk via Drive for desktop.
On each laptop, confirm in Finder / Explorer that the shared folder is fully downloaded (i.e., no cloud icon next to file names).
Open the lab folder in Finder (macOS) or Explorer (Windows). It shows up wherever Drive mounts (see "Where the folder lives on disk" below).
Right-click the folder. The Drive menu adds an Offline access submenu.
Click Available offline . The folder gets a small green check icon, and Drive starts pulling every file inside it down to disk.
Wait for the green check on every file inside. Cloud icons mean the file isn't local yet. ResearchOS won't be able to read those until they download.
Open ResearchOS, click Link Folder , and pick that shared folder.

If you want all of Drive locally (not just the lab folder), open Drive for desktop's Preferences → My Drive → Mirror files and confirm. Drive will download everything you have in My Drive over the next few minutes or hours depending on size. Pick this if you don't mind the ext...

entries.title: iCloud Drive
headings: Or keep all of iCloud Drive local
bodySnippets: iCloud Drive Optimize Storage offloads unused files. Mark the ResearchOS folder Keep Downloaded so it stays local.
On macOS, open System Settings → Apple ID → iCloud → iCloud Drive . Make sure it's turned on, and that the Desktop & Documents Folders sub-option is enabled if you plan to put the lab folder under .

Optimize Mac Storage (System Settings → Apple ID → iCloud) will re-offload files macOS decides you haven't used recently. Keep Downloaded overrides this per folder, so make sure it's set on the lab folder, not just on individual files.
Permissions on shared collaborations are tracked by Apple ID. If someone's share is revoked, ResearchOS will see empty or unreadable files. Reaccept the share via the email link.
Hidden placeholder files appear if the file is online-only. If you see instead of , the file isn't local. Run Keep Downloaded again.
Use iCloud Drive on your Mac (Apple Support)
Collaborate on folders with iCloud Drive (Apple Support)
One member creates an empty folder inside their iCloud Drive (e.g., ).
Right-click the folder in Finder → Share → Collaborate . Set permissions to Only invited people and Can make changes .
Send the share link to each lab member. They accept it on their Mac, and the folder appears in their iCloud Drive.
In Finder, navigate to the shared lab folder under iCloud Drive.
Right-click the folder and choose Keep Downloaded (it's near the bottom of the context menu). Every file inside downloads and the small cloud-with-arrow icon disappears.
Wait for the Finder sidebar's iCloud status to read "Up to date".
Open ResearchOS, click Link Folder , and pick that shared folder.

iCloud Drive technically runs on Windows via the iCloud for Windows app, but the experience is rough and Apple-specific permission quirks can break ResearchOS's folder access. If your lab has a mix of Mac and Windows users, we recommend OneDrive or Google Drive instead.
The folder picker can take up to a minute to open for iCloud Drive folders. This is normal; iCloud needs time to enumerate the folder tree. Wait for it rather than clicking again.

If you'd rather not have macOS offloading anything in iCloud, open System Settings -> Apple ID -> iCloud and turn off Optimize Mac Storage . Every file in your iCloud Drive stays on disk from then on. Reasonable if you have plenty of disk space and other iCloud folders that al...

entries.title: OneDrive
headings: Make it Always keep on this device (every member, every laptop)
Folder picker may be slow on Windows
Or keep all of OneDrive local
bodySnippets: OneDrive's Files On-Demand keeps files in the cloud by default. You need to mark the ResearchOS folder Always keep on this device.
Windows 10/11 : pre-installed. Sign in via Settings → Accounts.
macOS : download from microsoft.com/onedrive/download .

Storage Sense on Windows can auto-unpin files you haven't opened recently (i.e., move them back to cloud-only). Open Settings → System → Storage → Storage Sense and either disable the "content not opened in N days" option for the ResearchOS folder, or turn Storage Sense off en...
Cloud-only re-tag happens if you right-click and choose Free up space by accident. The folder reverts to cloud-only and ResearchOS can't read it. Re-run Always keep on this device .
Mac users : confirm the option says "Always keep on this device" (Files On-Demand). The older OneDrive Mac client stored files locally by default, but newer versions don't.
OneDrive Files On-Demand (Microsoft Support)
Share OneDrive files and folders (Microsoft Support)
One member creates an empty folder inside their OneDrive (e.g., ).
Right-click the folder → OneDrive → Share . Invite the other lab members by email and give them Edit permission.

Each invited member accepts the share. The folder appears under OneDrive -> Shared in the File Explorer left nav (Windows) or under your OneDrive folder in Finder (macOS). Using Add shortcut to My files is the most reliable way to get the shared folder into your normal OneDriv...
Open File Explorer (Windows) or Finder (macOS) and navigate to the shared lab folder.
Right-click the folder. In the context menu, choose Always keep on this device . A green check icon replaces the cloud icon on the folder and on every file inside it.
Wait until OneDrive finishes downloading everything (status "Up to date" in the menu-bar / tray icon).
Open ResearchOS, click Link Folder , and pick that shared folder.
The folder picker can take up to a minute to open on Windows for OneDrive folders. This is normal; OneDrive needs time to enumerate the folder tree. Wait for it rather than clicking again.

Shared folders sometimes show up as SharePoint libraries or in a separate "Shared with me" section. Microsoft's Add shortcut to My files button is the cleanest way to get the shared folder into your normal OneDrive tree, where Always keep on this device works reliably.

If you'd rather have your whole OneDrive on disk (not just the lab folder), click the OneDrive icon in the menu-bar / tray → Settings -> Sync and backup -> Advanced settings -> Files On-Demand and switch to Download all OneDrive files now . Pick this if you have other OneDrive...

entries.title: Oh god, another massive docs site
headings: What ResearchOS is, in one paragraph
The 7 things worth knowing up front
If you are brand new, start here
If you are setting up a shared lab folder
1. Your data is yours, on your machine
2. The “?” button is your shortcut into this wiki
3. BeakerBot will walk you through the app on first login
4. Home is for status. The Workbench is for doing experiments.
5. Tasks live in projects, projects live in your account
6. PIs (Principal Investigators) get a Lab Overview surface
7. Integrations (Telegram, calendar, AI Helper) are all opt-in
bodySnippets: Yeah, fair. There are a lot of pages in here. Good news: this is an encyclopedia, not a textbook. You are not supposed to read it cover to cover. You are supposed to land back here whenever you want to know how some specific thing works, find the page for it, skim, and leave.

If you only read one wiki page, read this one. It covers the small handful of things that make the difference between "I tried it once and bounced" and "I am fluent in this app." Then it tells you how to find the rest of the wiki when you actually need it.

A research project management app that runs entirely in your browser and reads / writes a folder on your own computer. No accounts, no sign-up, no upload. You point it at a folder, pick a username, and it stores everything (projects, tasks, lab notes, methods, PCR protocols, p...
The facts that matter most. Absorb these and you can pick up the rest as you go.
The wiki has dozens of pages because the app has dozens of features. Three ways to navigate it, in order of speed:

Each page is built concept-first. The top of the page explains what the feature actually is and why it exists. Below that are screenshots and procedural steps. You can usually skim to the screenshot of the thing you need and stop there.
Walk these in order (each is short):

Read Shared Lab Accounts first. Putting the ResearchOS folder inside OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, Box, or iCloud lets your whole lab work out of one folder. The catch: you have to configure the sync client to keep the folder downloaded locally rather than on-demand. If you...

This app is local-first and single-developer, and the wiki tries to be honest about what works and what does not yet. If something is broken or confusing, the Feedback page in the app files a GitHub issue with the context pre-filled. It's the fastest way to get things fixed.
Type a phrase in the search bar at the top of the sidebar. Fastest. If you know the word for the thing you want ("gradient," "telegram," "edit session"), search beats browsing every time.

Browse by category in the sidebar on the left. Pages are grouped under Getting Started, Shared Lab Accounts, Features, Integrations, and Security. If you know roughly what area you are in, this works well.

Hit the "?" button inside the app. Bounces you straight to the wiki page for whatever surface you were just looking at. This is the right move when you are mid-task and want context, not when you are exploring.
Browser requirements . Chrome or Edge. The folder API does not work in Safari, Firefox, or Brave.
Connecting your folder . Picking the folder ResearchOS reads and writes.
Creating a user . Username, optional password.
Welcome tour (BeakerBot) . What the guided walkthrough covers.
If you want a sandbox first, try the demo (in-browser, fake yeast lab, edits vanish on reload).

Everything you do writes to the folder you connected. Nothing uploads. There is no ResearchOS server holding your research. If you want to back it up, copy the folder. If you want to walk away, delete it. The only network calls the app makes are to fetch your own Telegram phot...

Top-right of every page in the app. Click it and it opens the wiki page for whatever surface you were just looking at. Confused about a colored bar on the Gantt? Hit "?" and you land on the Gantt wiki page.

A guided tour auto-starts the first time you sign in. It is roughly 20 minutes, hands-on, in your actual account. You can skip it any time and re-run it later from Settings → Walkthrough . (Separate thing: the folder-picker screen also has a small Take the 3-minute walkthrough...

Two surfaces, two purposes. Home is the dashboard you glance at each morning: today's tasks, upcoming deadlines, recent activity. The Workbench is where you actually run experiments: three tabs (Experiments, Notes, Lists), a project filter that sticks across tabs, and a popup ...

Hierarchy: account → projects → tasks. Sharing happens at the task level (or implicitly through shared methods). When you share a task with a labmate, they see it in their own Workbench under the project, with editing rights if you grant them. The unit of collaboration is the ...

If your account is flagged as a PI , you unlock Lab Overview , a fixed, curated page with cross-member views (member workload, recent activity, purchase queue, audit log). It is not visible to regular members. Set the PI flag on your account to get the bird's-eye view. See PI .

ResearchOS works completely standalone. The Telegram bot (snap a photo on your phone, it lands in your inbox), the calendar feeds (subscribe to a Google / Outlook / iCloud calendar), and the AI Helper are all in Settings, all off by default, and all separately switchable. Conn...

entries.title: Trust
headings: 1. Your data never leaves your machine
2. The science is validated against tools labs already trust
bodySnippets: Trust is not a feeling you can market your way into. It is a set of claims a lab can check. This section lays out the four claims ResearchOS makes about itself and points you at the public, verifiable evidence behind each one.

Picking an electronic lab notebook means handing it years of irreplaceable work. That is a big ask, and the honest answer to "why should I trust this" should never be "because we said so." ResearchOS is built so that the important promises are things you can confirm yourself, ...
There are four pillars. Each one has its own page below, and most of them link out to a live, public page that anyone can open without an account.

ResearchOS reads and writes a folder you pick on your own computer. There is no database we control, so there is nothing for us to lose, sell, or get breached. The Security section is the full plain-English version of that claim, including the narrow exceptions and how to veri...

Every sequence and lab calculation ResearchOS performs is checked, on every commit, against the peer-reviewed reference tools the field already relies on (Biopython, primer3, pydna). The numbers cannot silently drift, because a drift fails the build. Method validation explains...

ResearchOS is licensed under AGPLv3, which means a lab can read the whole thing, fork it, and self-host it. The project also credits and thanks the open-source work it builds on. Open source and license covers what the license gives you and where the credits live.

ResearchOS is grant-funded through a UW-Madison RISE fellowship, not venture-backed. There is no paid tier and no per-seat fee, which is a deliberate choice to keep the tool reachable for low-resource labs. How it stays free explains the funding model honestly.
Your data stays with you, the math is checked against the references your field trusts, the code is open for anyone to read or run, and the money behind the project does not depend on locking you in.

entries.title: How it stays free
headings: What that means for your lab
Why this keeps it reachable for low-resource labs
What we are careful not to promise
bodySnippets: Who pays for a tool shapes who it serves. ResearchOS is grant-funded, not venture-backed, and that single fact is why it can stay free and open for labs that could never afford a per-seat subscription.

ResearchOS is funded by a UW-Madison RISE fellowship. It is not backed by venture capital, it is not chasing an acquisition, and it does not answer to investors who need the user base monetized. The work is paid for as research infrastructure, which is a fundamentally differen...
Because the funding does not depend on extracting payment from the people using the tool, ResearchOS does not have the usual levers a commercial ELN reaches for.

A per-seat ELN subscription is a real barrier. For a small lab, a teaching lab, a lab at an under-resourced institution, or a researcher in a setting where the field's standard tools are simply out of budget, the price is often the reason good data management never happens. Gr...

It would be easy, and dishonest, to promise the project will never cost anything under any circumstances. No one can guarantee the funding landscape years out. What ResearchOS can say plainly is the commitment behind the design. The project is built to stay free and open for t...
No paid tier. There is no premium version that unlocks the features you actually need.
No per-seat fees. Adding a student or a collaborator does not raise a bill, so the cost of the tool does not grow with the size of your lab.
Open by license. ResearchOS is AGPLv3, so even if you never pay anything, you keep the right to read, fork, and self-host the code. The copyright is held by the author, Grant R. Nickles.

ResearchOS is free and open, grant-funded today, with donations welcome to help sustain it over time. The grant covers the near term, and voluntary support can extend the runway without ever turning the tool into a product that charges low-resource labs to use it.

entries.title: Method validation
headings: Why the numbers cannot silently drift
bodySnippets: An ELN that gets a melting temperature wrong is worse than no ELN at all, because you would trust the wrong number. ResearchOS answers that worry by checking its own math, on every commit, against the published tools your field already relies on.

ResearchOS runs its sequence and lab calculations in your browser. Melting temperature, sequence alignment, restriction digest, translation, protein parameters, the lab calculators, and the cloning engine all execute locally on your machine, with no server round-trip and no pr...

The answer is that ResearchOS does not ask you to take its math on faith. For each capability, it compares its own output against a peer-reviewed reference implementation that the field already trusts. These are not numbers we invented. They come from the same tools working sc...
The reference tools, called oracles in the code, are the established names in computational biology.

For every showcase case, ResearchOS records its own value, the pinned oracle value, the exact tool version, the specific function called, and the committed script that re-derives the reference number. A reader can follow that trail and reproduce it.

The honest part is not just that ResearchOS matches these tools once. It is that the match is re-checked automatically and can never quietly fall out of agreement. The public Method validation page and the test that gates the build both call the exact same function, in . The p...

That gating test, , runs on every commit. If a future change to a ResearchOS calculation pushes its output past the agreed tolerance for any case, the comparison fails, the test fails, and the build fails before the change can reach you. A documented, explained difference (for...

Matching a reference tool means ResearchOS computes the same thing the reference computes, faithfully. It does not mean the underlying method is the only valid one, and it does not replace your own judgment about which method fits your experiment. The point is narrower and mor...
Biopython for melting temperature, alignment, restriction digest, translation, and protein parameters (molecular weight, isoelectric point, and related properties).
primer3 for nearest-neighbor melting temperature, the workhorse behind most primer-design pipelines.
pydna for cloning and assembly products, so a Gibson or Gateway result can be checked against a published reference sequence.

The Method validation page is public and needs no account. It lists every comparison, our number next to theirs, the delta, the tolerance, and the source script. No sign-in, no demo data, just the agreement table.
The public Method validation page. Every comparison is counted (exact, within tolerance, or a documented difference), and the cases that diverge are spotlighted rather than hidden.
The public Method validation page summarizing 146 exact matches, 8 within a documented tolerance, and 2 larger documented differences, with a differences spotlight calling out the cases that diverge.

entries.title: Open source and license
headings: Crediting the work we build on
bodySnippets: Closed software asks you to trust a promise. Open software lets you trust the source. ResearchOS is licensed under AGPLv3, which means a lab can read the whole codebase, fork it, and run its own copy.
ResearchOS is released under the GNU Affero General Public License, version 3 (AGPLv3). In plain terms, that license gives every lab three durable rights.

The copyleft nature of AGPLv3 also means those rights travel forward. Anyone who distributes a modified version, including over a network, has to offer the same freedoms to their users. The openness is not a one-time gift, it is a property the license keeps enforcing.

ResearchOS does not exist in a vacuum. It is built on top of a large body of open-source software and published science, and the project takes the obligation to credit that work seriously, both as a matter of courtesy and as a matter of license.

The public Built on open source page opens with a sincere thank-you to the open-source and scientific community, then credits the specific projects ResearchOS depends on. It groups the highlights by area, calls out the code vendored directly (such as SeqViz, the TeselaGen tg-o...

Many open-source licenses require attribution as a condition of use. MIT and BSD code must carry its copyright notice, and Apache-2.0 code requires a NOTICE. ResearchOS satisfies those requirements in the file at the repository root, which lists every third-party package it sh...
Read it. The entire codebase is public. Nothing about how ResearchOS stores your data, computes a melting temperature, or talks to the network is hidden behind a binary.
Fork it. You can take the code, change it, and build the version your lab needs. You are not waiting on a vendor's roadmap.
Self-host it. You can run your own copy on your own infrastructure. If the hosted version ever disappeared, the project could not be taken away from the labs already running it.

A weaker license would let someone take ResearchOS, lock it behind a paywall, and never share their changes back. AGPLv3 is the version that closes the network-service loophole, so a lab-grade tool stays open even when it is offered as a service.
The Built on open source page is public and needs no account. It is the warm, human-readable companion to the machine-generated notices file in the repo.
